title: First World War

infobox.key: See: [Main Allied leaders](/wiki/Allied_leaders_of_World_War_I "Allied leaders of World War I")
infobox.value: See: [Main Central Powers leaders](/wiki/Leaders_of_the_Central_Powers_of_World_War_I "Leaders of the Central Powers of World War I")

* [France](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic") * [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland")  and [Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"):   * [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") * [Canada](/wiki/Canada_in_World_War_I "Canada in World War I") * [Ceylon](/wiki/British_Ceylon "British Ceylon") * [Egypt](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Egypt "Sultanate of Egypt") * [Newfoundland](/wiki/Dominion_of_Newfoundland "Dominion of Newfoundland") * [New Zealand](/wiki/Dominion_of_New_Zealand "Dominion of New Zealand") * [India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") * [South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa")  * [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") (until 1917)[[a]](#cite_note-1) * [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") (from 1915) * [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") (from 1917) * [Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") [and others ...](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") | **[Central Powers](/wiki/Central_Powers "Central Powers"):** * [Germany](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") * [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") * [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") * [Bulgaria](/wiki/Tsardom_of_Bulgaria_(1908%E2%80%931946) "Tsardom of Bulgaria (1908–1946)") (from 1915) [and others ...](/wiki/Central_Powers "Central Powers") |

(28 July 1914 – 11 November 1918), also known as the **Great War**, was a [global conflict](/wiki/World_war "World war") between two [coalitions](/wiki/Coalition "Coalition"): the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") (or Entente) and the [Central Powers](/wiki/Central_Powers "Central Powers"). Major areas of conflict included [Europe](/wiki/European_theatre_of_World_War_I "European theatre of World War I") and the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_Eastern_theatre_of_World_War_I "Middle Eastern theatre of World War I"), as well as parts of [Africa](/wiki/African_theatre_of_World_War_I "African theatre of World War I") and the [Asia-Pacific](/wiki/Asian_and_Pacific_theatre_of_World_War_I "Asian and Pacific theatre of World War I"). The war saw important developments in weaponry including [tanks](/wiki/Tanks_in_World_War_I "Tanks in World War I"), [aircraft](/wiki/Aviation_in_World_War_I "Aviation in World War I"), [artillery](/wiki/Artillery_of_World_War_I "Artillery of World War I"), machine guns, and [chemical weapons](/wiki/Chemical_weapons_in_World_War_I "Chemical weapons in World War I"). One of the [deadliest conflicts in history](/wiki/List_of_wars_by_death_toll "List of wars by death toll"), it resulted in an estimated [15 to 22 million military and civilian casualties](/wiki/World_War_I_casualties "World War I casualties") and [genocide](/wiki/Genocides_in_history_(World_War_I_through_World_War_II) "Genocides in history (World War I through World War II)"). The movement of large numbers of people was a major factor in the deadly [Spanish flu](/wiki/Spanish_flu "Spanish flu") pandemic.

**World War I**,[[b]](#cite_note-2) or the **First World War** (28 July 1914 – 11 November 1918), also known as the **Great War**, was a [global conflict](/wiki/World_war "World war") between two [coalitions](/wiki/Coalition "Coalition"): the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") (or Entente) and the [Central Powers](/wiki/Central_Powers "Central Powers"). Major areas of conflict included [Europe](/wiki/European_theatre_of_World_War_I "European theatre of World War I") and the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_Eastern_theatre_of_World_War_I "Middle Eastern theatre of World War I"), as well as parts of [Africa](/wiki/African_theatre_of_World_War_I "African theatre of World War I") and the [Asia-Pacific](/wiki/Asian_and_Pacific_theatre_of_World_War_I "Asian and Pacific theatre of World War I"). The war saw important developments in weaponry including [tanks](/wiki/Tanks_in_World_War_I "Tanks in World War I"), [aircraft](/wiki/Aviation_in_World_War_I "Aviation in World War I"), [artillery](/wiki/Artillery_of_World_War_I "Artillery of World War I"), machine guns, and [chemical weapons](/wiki/Chemical_weapons_in_World_War_I "Chemical weapons in World War I"). One of the [deadliest conflicts in history](/wiki/List_of_wars_by_death_toll "List of wars by death toll"), it resulted in an estimated [15 to 22 million military and civilian casualties](/wiki/World_War_I_casualties "World War I casualties") and [genocide](/wiki/Genocides_in_history_(World_War_I_through_World_War_II) "Genocides in history (World War I through World War II)"). The movement of large numbers of people was a major factor in the deadly [Spanish flu](/wiki/Spanish_flu "Spanish flu") pandemic.

The [causes of World War I](/wiki/Causes_of_World_War_I "Causes of World War I") included the rise of the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") and [decline of the Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Decline_and_modernization_of_the_Ottoman_Empire "Decline and modernization of the Ottoman Empire"), which disturbed the long-standing [balance of power](/wiki/Balance_of_power_(international_relations) "Balance of power (international relations)") in Europe, the [exacerbation of imperial rivalries](/wiki/New_Imperialism "New Imperialism"), and an arms race between the [great powers](/wiki/Great_power "Great power"). Growing tensions in the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") reached [a breaking point](/wiki/July_Crisis "July Crisis") on 28 June 1914 when [Gavrilo Princip](/wiki/Gavrilo_Princip "Gavrilo Princip"), a [Bosnian Serb](/wiki/Bosnian_Serb "Bosnian Serb"), [assassinated Franz Ferdinand](/wiki/Assassination_of_Archduke_Franz_Ferdinand "Assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand"), the heir to the Austro-Hungarian throne. [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") blamed [Serbia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbia "Kingdom of Serbia"), and declared war on 28 July. After [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") mobilised in Serbia's defence, Germany declared war on Russia and [France](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic"), who had [an alliance](/wiki/Franco-Russian_Alliance "Franco-Russian Alliance"). The [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") entered the war after Germany [invaded Belgium](/wiki/German_invasion_of_Belgium_(1914) "German invasion of Belgium (1914)"), and the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") joined the Central Powers in November. [Germany's strategy in 1914](/wiki/Schlieffen_Plan "Schlieffen Plan") was to quickly defeat France before transferring its forces to the east, but its advance [was halted in September](/wiki/First_Battle_of_the_Marne "First Battle of the Marne"), and by the end of the year the [Western Front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)") consisted of [a near-continuous line of trenches](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I)#Trench_warfare "Western Front (World War I)") from the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel") to [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"). The [Eastern Front](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_I) "Eastern Front (World War I)") was more dynamic, but neither side gained a decisive advantage, despite costly offensives. [Italy](/wiki/Military_history_of_Italy_during_World_War_I "Military history of Italy during World War I"), [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria_during_World_War_I "Bulgaria during World War I"), [Romania](/wiki/Romania_in_World_War_I "Romania in World War I"), [Greece](/wiki/Greece_during_World_War_I "Greece during World War I"), and others entered the war from 1915 onward.

Major battles, including those at [Verdun](/wiki/Battle_of_Verdun "Battle of Verdun"), [the Somme](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Somme "Battle of the Somme"), and [Passchendaele](/wiki/Battle_of_Passchendaele "Battle of Passchendaele"), failed to break the stalemate on the Western Front. In April 1917, the [United States joined the Allies](/wiki/American_entry_into_World_War_I "American entry into World War I") after Germany resumed [unrestricted submarine warfare](/wiki/Unrestricted_submarine_warfare_(February_1917) "Unrestricted submarine warfare (February 1917)") against Atlantic shipping. Later that year, the [Bolsheviks](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks") seized power in Russia in the [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution"); [Soviet Russia](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic") signed [an armistice](/wiki/Armistice_between_Russia_and_the_Central_Powers "Armistice between Russia and the Central Powers") with the Central Powers in December, followed by [a separate peace](/wiki/Treaty_of_Brest-Litovsk "Treaty of Brest-Litovsk") in March 1918. That month, Germany launched [a spring offensive in the west](/wiki/German_spring_offensive "German spring offensive"), which despite initial successes left the [German Army](/wiki/Imperial_German_Army_in_World_War_I "Imperial German Army in World War I") exhausted and demoralised. The Allied [Hundred Days Offensive](/wiki/Hundred_Days_Offensive "Hundred Days Offensive"), beginning in August 1918, caused a collapse of the German front line. Following the [Vardar Offensive](/wiki/Vardar_Offensive "Vardar Offensive"), Bulgaria signed [an armistice](/wiki/Armistice_of_Salonica "Armistice of Salonica") in late September. By early November, the Allies had signed armistices [with the Ottomans](/wiki/Armistice_of_Mudros "Armistice of Mudros") and [with Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Armistice_of_Villa_Giusti "Armistice of Villa Giusti"), leaving Germany isolated. Facing [a revolution at home](/wiki/German_revolution_of_1918%E2%80%931919 "German revolution of 1918–1919"), Kaiser [Wilhelm II](/wiki/Wilhelm_II "Wilhelm II") [abdicated](/wiki/Abdication_of_Wilhelm_II "Abdication of Wilhelm II") on 9 November, and the war ended with the [Armistice of 11 November 1918](/wiki/Armistice_of_11_November_1918 "Armistice of 11 November 1918").

The [Paris Peace Conference](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Conference_(1919%E2%80%931920) "Paris Peace Conference (1919–1920)") of 1919–1920 imposed settlements on the defeated powers. Under the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles"), Germany lost significant territories, was disarmed, and was required to pay large [war reparations](/wiki/War_reparations "War reparations") to the Allies. The dissolution of the Russian, German, Austro-Hungarian, and Ottoman empires led to new national boundaries and the creation of new independent states including [Poland](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), the [Baltic states](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states"), [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/First_Czechoslovak_Republic "First Czechoslovak Republic"), and [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia"). The [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") was established to maintain world peace, but its failure to manage instability during the [interwar period](/wiki/Interwar_period "Interwar period") contributed to the outbreak of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") in 1939.

## Names

Before [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the events of 1914–1918 were commonly referred to as the ***Great War*** or simply the ***World War***.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeonhard20183-3) In August 1914, *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent_(New_York_City) "The Independent (New York City)")* stated of the conflict, "This is the Great War. It names itself."[[2]](#cite_note-independent19140817-4) In the decade after its end, many hoped it would be "[the war to end all wars](/wiki/The_war_to_end_war "The war to end war")," because of its unprecedented destructiveness and death toll.[[3]](#cite_note-5) The earliest known use of the term *First World War* appeared in September 1914, when German biologist and philosopher [Ernst Haeckel](/wiki/Ernst_Haeckel "Ernst Haeckel") wrote that the ongoing "European War" would become "the first world war in the full sense of the word."[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShapiroEpstein2006329-6)

## Background

Main article: [Causes of World War I](/wiki/Causes_of_World_War_I "Causes of World War I")

### Political and military alliances

[![Map of Europe focusing on Austria-Hungary and marking the central location of ethnic groups in it including Slovaks, Czechs, Slovenes, Croats, Serbs, Romanians, Ukrainians, Poles.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Map_Europe_alliances_1914-en.svg/250px-Map_Europe_alliances_1914-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_Europe_alliances_1914-en.svg)

Rival military coalitions in 1914:[[c]](#cite_note-7)

[Triple Entente](/wiki/Triple_Entente "Triple Entente")

[Triple Alliance](/wiki/Triple_Alliance_(1882) "Triple Alliance (1882)")

For much of the 19th century, the major European powers maintained a tenuous [balance of power](/wiki/Balance_of_power_(international_relations) "Balance of power (international relations)"), known as the [Concert of Europe](/wiki/Concert_of_Europe "Concert of Europe").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark2013121–152-8) After 1848, this was challenged by Britain's withdrawal into [splendid isolation](/wiki/Splendid_isolation "Splendid isolation"), the [decline of the Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Decline_and_modernization_of_the_Ottoman_Empire "Decline and modernization of the Ottoman Empire"), [New Imperialism](/wiki/New_Imperialism "New Imperialism"), and the rise of [Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") under [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck"). Victory in the 1870–1871 [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War") allowed Bismarck to [consolidate](/wiki/Unification_of_Germany "Unification of Germany") a [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire"). After 1871, French policy aimed to [avenge](/wiki/Revanchism "Revanchism") this defeat[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZeldin1977117-9)[[7]](#cite_note-10) and expand [France's colonial empire](/wiki/French_colonial_empire "French colonial empire").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJoly1999325–347-11)

In 1873, Bismarck negotiated the [League of the Three Emperors](/wiki/League_of_the_Three_Emperors "League of the Three Emperors"), which included [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"), [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), and Germany. After the 1877–1878 [Russo-Turkish War](/wiki/Russo-Turkish_War_(1877%E2%80%931878) "Russo-Turkish War (1877–1878)"), the League was dissolved due to Austrian concerns over the expansion of Russian influence in the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans"), an area they considered to be of vital strategic interest. [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") and Austria-Hungary then formed the 1879 [Dual Alliance](/wiki/Dual_Alliance_(1879) "Dual Alliance (1879)"), which became the [Triple Alliance](/wiki/Triple_Alliance_(1882) "Triple Alliance (1882)") when Italy joined in 1882.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeegan199852-12) For Bismarck, the purpose of these agreements was to isolate France by ensuring the three empires resolved any disputes among themselves. In 1887, Bismarck set up the [Reinsurance Treaty](/wiki/Reinsurance_Treaty "Reinsurance Treaty"), a secret agreement between Germany and Russia to remain neutral if either were attacked by France or Austria-Hungary.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMedlicott194566–70-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/World_1914_empires_colonies_territory.PNG/500px-World_1914_empires_colonies_territory.PNG)](/wiki/File:World_1914_empires_colonies_territory.PNG)

World empires and colonies c. 1914

For Bismarck, peace with Russia was the foundation of German foreign policy, but in 1890, he was forced to retire by [Wilhelm II](/wiki/Wilhelm_II "Wilhelm II"). The latter was persuaded not to renew the Reinsurance Treaty by his new [Chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany "Chancellor of Germany"), [Leo von Caprivi](/wiki/Leo_von_Caprivi "Leo von Caprivi").[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennan198620-14) This gave France an opening to agree to the [Franco-Russian Alliance](/wiki/Franco-Russian_Alliance "Franco-Russian Alliance") in 1894, which was then followed by the 1904 *[Entente Cordiale](/wiki/Entente_Cordiale "Entente Cordiale")* with Britain. The [Triple Entente](/wiki/Triple_Entente "Triple Entente") was completed by the 1907 [Anglo-Russian Convention](/wiki/Anglo-Russian_Convention "Anglo-Russian Convention"). While not formal alliances, by settling [longstanding colonial disputes in Asia](/wiki/Great_Game "Great Game") and Africa, British support for France or Russia in any future conflict became a possibility.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWillmott200315-15) This was accentuated by British and Russian support for France against Germany during the 1911 [Agadir Crisis](/wiki/Agadir_Crisis "Agadir Crisis").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFay1930290–293-16)

### Arms race

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Bundesarchiv_DVM_10_Bild-23-61-23%2C_Linienschiff_%22SMS_Rheinland%22.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_DVM_10_Bild-23-61-23%2C_Linienschiff_%22SMS_Rheinland%22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_DVM_10_Bild-23-61-23,_Linienschiff_%22SMS_Rheinland%22.jpg)

[SMS *Rheinland*](/wiki/SMS_Rheinland "SMS Rheinland"), a [*Nassau*-class](/wiki/Nassau-class_battleship "Nassau-class battleship") battleship, Germany's first response to the British *Dreadnought*, 1910

German economic and industrial strength continued to expand rapidly post-1871. Backed by [Wilhelm II](/wiki/Wilhelm_II "Wilhelm II"), Admiral [Alfred von Tirpitz](/wiki/Alfred_von_Tirpitz "Alfred von Tirpitz") sought to use this growth to build an [Imperial German Navy](/wiki/Imperial_German_Navy "Imperial German Navy") that could compete with the British [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWillmott200321-17) This policy was based on the work of US naval author [Alfred Thayer Mahan](/wiki/Alfred_Thayer_Mahan "Alfred Thayer Mahan"), who argued that possession of a [blue-water navy](/wiki/Blue-water_navy "Blue-water navy") was vital for global power projection; Tirpitz had his books translated into German, while Wilhelm made them required reading for his advisors and senior military personnel.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerwig198872–73-18)

Bismarck opposed any attempt to compete with the Royal Navy, since he believed Britain would not interfere in Europe as long as its maritime supremacy remained secure. His dismissal in 1890 led to a change in policy and the start of an [Anglo-German naval arms race](/wiki/Anglo-German_naval_arms_race "Anglo-German naval arms race").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMollLuebbert1980153–185-19) Despite the vast sums spent by Tirpitz, the launch of [HMS *Dreadnought*](/wiki/HMS_Dreadnought_(1906) "HMS Dreadnought (1906)") in 1906 made every existing battleship obsolete, and gave the British a technological advantage they never relinquished.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWillmott200321-17) Ultimately, Germany invested huge resources on creating a navy large enough to antagonise Britain, but not defeat it. In 1911, Chancellor [Theobald von Bethmann Hollweg](/wiki/Theobald_von_Bethmann_Hollweg "Theobald von Bethmann Hollweg") acknowledged defeat, leading to the *Rüstungswende* or 'armaments turning point', when he switched expenditure from the navy to the army.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStevenson201645-20)

This decision was driven by German concerns over the speed of Russia's recovery from defeat in the [Russo-Japanese War](/wiki/Russo-Japanese_War "Russo-Japanese War") and the subsequent [1905 Russian Revolution](/wiki/1905_Russian_Revolution "1905 Russian Revolution"). Economic reforms led to a significant post-1908 expansion of railways and transportation infrastructure, particularly in its western border regions.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrisp1976174–196-21) Since Germany and Austria-Hungary relied on faster mobilisation to compensate for their numerical inferiority compared to Russia, the threat posed by the closing of this gap was more important than competing with the Royal Navy. After Germany expanded its standing army by 170,000 troops in 1913, France extended compulsory military service from two to three years, sparking similar measures from the [Balkan powers](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans"), Italy, the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") and Austria-Hungary. Absolute figures are difficult to calculate due to differences in categorising expenditure since they often omit civilian infrastructure projects like railways which had logistical importance and military use. However, from 1908 to 1913, military spending by the six major European powers increased by over 50% in real terms.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStevenson201642-22)

### Conflicts in the Balkans

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Austria_Hungary_ethnic.svg/330px-Austria_Hungary_ethnic.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Austria_Hungary_ethnic.svg)

Ethno-linguistic map of Austria-Hungary, 1910. [Bosnia-Herzegovina was annexed](/wiki/Bosnian_Crisis "Bosnian Crisis") in 1908.

The years before 1914 were marked by a series of crises in the Balkans, as other powers sought to benefit from the [Ottoman decline](/wiki/Ottoman_decline_thesis "Ottoman decline thesis"). While [Pan-Slavic](/wiki/Pan-Slavism "Pan-Slavism") and [Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") Russia considered itself the protector of [Serbia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbia "Kingdom of Serbia") and other [Slav](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs") states, they preferred the strategically vital [Bosporus](/wiki/Bosporus "Bosporus") straits to be controlled by a weak Ottoman government, rather than an ambitious Slav power like [Bulgaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bulgaria "Kingdom of Bulgaria"). Russia had ambitions in northeastern [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia") while its clients had overlapping claims in the Balkans. These competing interests divided Russian policy-makers and added to regional instability.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcMeekin201566–67-23)

Austrian statesmen viewed the Balkans as essential for the continued existence of their Empire and saw Serbian expansion as a direct threat. The 1908–1909 [Bosnian Crisis](/wiki/Bosnian_Crisis "Bosnian Crisis") began when Austria annexed the former Ottoman territory of [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_Vilayet "Bosnia Vilayet"), which it [had occupied](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_campaign_in_Bosnia_and_Herzegovina_in_1878 "Austro-Hungarian campaign in Bosnia and Herzegovina in 1878") since 1878. Timed to coincide with the [Bulgarian Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Bulgarian_Declaration_of_Independence "Bulgarian Declaration of Independence") from the Ottoman Empire, this unilateral action was denounced by the European powers, but accepted as there was no consensus on how to resolve the situation. Some historians see this as a significant escalation, ending any chance of Austria cooperating with Russia in the Balkans, while also damaging diplomatic relations between Serbia and Italy.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark201386-24)

Tensions increased after the 1911–1912 [Italo-Turkish War](/wiki/Italo-Turkish_War "Italo-Turkish War") demonstrated Ottoman weakness and led to the formation of the [Balkan League](/wiki/Balkan_League "Balkan League"), an alliance of Serbia, Bulgaria, [Montenegro](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Montenegro "Kingdom of Montenegro"), and [Greece](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Greece "Kingdom of Greece").[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark2013251–252-25) The League quickly overran most of the Ottomans' territory in the Balkans during the 1912–1913 [First Balkan War](/wiki/First_Balkan_War "First Balkan War"), much to the surprise of outside observers.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcMeekin201569-26) The Serbian capture of ports on the [Adriatic](/wiki/Albanian_Adriatic_Sea_Coast "Albanian Adriatic Sea Coast") resulted in partial Austrian mobilisation, starting on 21 November 1912, including units along the Russian border in [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Eastern_Europe) "Galicia (Eastern Europe)"). The [Russian government](/wiki/Council_of_Ministers_of_the_Russian_Empire "Council of Ministers of the Russian Empire") decided not to mobilise in response, unprepared to precipitate a war.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcMeekin201573-27)

The great powers sought to re-assert control through the 1913 [Treaty of London](/wiki/Treaty_of_London_(1913) "Treaty of London (1913)"), which had created an independent [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania") while enlarging the territories of Bulgaria, Serbia, Montenegro and Greece. However, disputes between the victors sparked the 33-day [Second Balkan War](/wiki/Second_Balkan_War "Second Balkan War"), when Bulgaria attacked Serbia and Greece on 16 June 1913; it was defeated, losing most of [Macedonia](/wiki/Macedonia_(region) "Macedonia (region)") to Serbia and Greece, and [Southern Dobruja](/wiki/Southern_Dobruja "Southern Dobruja") to Romania.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWillmott20032–23-28) The result was that even countries which benefited from the Balkan Wars, such as Serbia and Greece, felt cheated of their "rightful gains", while for Austria it demonstrated the apparent indifference with which other powers viewed their concerns, including Germany.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark2013288-29) This complex mix of resentment, nationalism and insecurity helps explain why the pre-1914 Balkans became known as the "[powder keg of Europe](/wiki/Powder_keg_of_Europe "Powder keg of Europe")".[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeegan199848–49-30)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBennett199541–59-31)[[29]](#cite_note-32)[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson1983717–718-33)

## Prelude

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of World War I](/wiki/Timeline_of_World_War_I "Timeline of World War I").

### Sarajevo assassination

Main article: [Assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand](/wiki/Assassination_of_Archduke_Franz_Ferdinand "Assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Ferdinand_Behr_arrested_in_Sarajevo_1914.jpg/250px-Ferdinand_Behr_arrested_in_Sarajevo_1914.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ferdinand_Behr_arrested_in_Sarajevo_1914.jpg)

Traditionally thought to show the arrest of [Gavrilo Princip](/wiki/Gavrilo_Princip "Gavrilo Princip") (right), [this photo](/wiki/Arrest_of_a_suspect_in_Sarajevo "Arrest of a suspect in Sarajevo") is now believed by historians to depict an innocent bystander, Ferdinand Behr, on 28 June 1914.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFinestone1981247-34)[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith2010He_was_photographed_on_the_way_to_the_station_and_the_photograph_has_been_reproduced_many_times_in_books_and_articles,_claiming_to_depict_the_arrest_of_Gavrilo_Princip._But_there_is_no_photograph_of_Gavro's_arrest—this_photograph_shows_the_arrest_of_Behr.-35)

On 28 June 1914, [Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria](/wiki/Archduke_Franz_Ferdinand_of_Austria "Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria"), heir presumptive to Emperor [Franz Joseph I of Austria](/wiki/Franz_Joseph_I_of_Austria "Franz Joseph I of Austria"), visited [Sarajevo](/wiki/Sarajevo "Sarajevo"), the capital of the [recently annexed Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Annexation_of_Bosnia "Annexation of Bosnia"). [Cvjetko Popović](/wiki/Cvjetko_Popovi%C4%87 "Cvjetko Popović"), [Gavrilo Princip](/wiki/Gavrilo_Princip "Gavrilo Princip"), [Nedeljko Čabrinović](/wiki/Nedeljko_%C4%8Cabrinovi%C4%87 "Nedeljko Čabrinović"), [Trifko Grabež](/wiki/Trifko_Grabe%C5%BE "Trifko Grabež"), [Vaso Čubrilović](/wiki/Vaso_%C4%8Cubrilovi%C4%87 "Vaso Čubrilović") ([Bosnian Serbs](/wiki/Serbs_of_Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Serbs of Bosnia and Herzegovina")) and [Muhamed Mehmedbašić](/wiki/Muhamed_Mehmedba%C5%A1i%C4%87 "Muhamed Mehmedbašić") (from the [Bosniaks](/wiki/Bosniaks "Bosniaks") community),[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEButcher2014103-36) from the movement known as [Young Bosnia](/wiki/Young_Bosnia "Young Bosnia"), took up positions along the Archduke's motorcade route, to assassinate him. Supplied with arms by extremists within the Serbian [Black Hand](/wiki/Black_Hand_(Serbia) "Black Hand (Serbia)") intelligence organisation, they hoped his death would free Bosnia from Austrian rule.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEButcher2014188–189-37)

Čabrinović threw a [grenade](/wiki/Grenade "Grenade") at the Archduke's car and injured two of his aides. The other assassins were also unsuccessful. An hour later, as Ferdinand was returning from visiting the injured officers in hospital, his car took a wrong turn into a street where [Gavrilo Princip](/wiki/Gavrilo_Princip "Gavrilo Princip") was standing. He fired two pistol shots, fatally wounding Ferdinand and his wife [Sophie](/wiki/Sophie,_Duchess_of_Hohenberg "Sophie, Duchess of Hohenberg").[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert199416-38)

According to historian [Zbyněk Zeman](/wiki/Zbyn%C4%9Bk_Zeman "Zbyněk Zeman"), in Vienna "the event almost failed to make any impression whatsoever. On 28 and 29 June, the crowds listened to music and drank wine, as if nothing had happened."[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWillmott200326-39) Nevertheless, the impact of the murder of the heir to the throne was significant, and has been described by historian [Christopher Clark](/wiki/Christopher_Clark "Christopher Clark") as a "9/11 effect, a terrorist event charged with historic meaning, transforming the political chemistry in Vienna".[[37]](#cite_note-Christopher_Clark_2014-40)

### Expansion of violence in Bosnia and Herzegovina

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/1914-06-29_-_Aftermath_of_attacks_against_Serbs_in_Sarajevo.png/250px-1914-06-29_-_Aftermath_of_attacks_against_Serbs_in_Sarajevo.png)](/wiki/File:1914-06-29_-_Aftermath_of_attacks_against_Serbs_in_Sarajevo.png)

Crowds on the streets in the aftermath of the [anti-Serb riots in Sarajevo](/wiki/Anti-Serb_riots_in_Sarajevo "Anti-Serb riots in Sarajevo"), 29 June 1914

Austro-Hungarian authorities encouraged subsequent [anti-Serb riots in Sarajevo](/wiki/Anti-Serb_riots_in_Sarajevo "Anti-Serb riots in Sarajevo").[[38]](#cite_note-DjordjevićSpence1992-41)[[39]](#cite_note-42) Violent actions against ethnic Serbs were also organised outside Sarajevo, in other cities in Austro-Hungarian-controlled Bosnia and Herzegovina, Croatia and Slovenia. Austro-Hungarian authorities in Bosnia and Herzegovina imprisoned approximately 5,500 prominent Serbs, 700 to 2,200 of whom died in prison. A further 460 Serbs were sentenced to death. A predominantly Bosniak special militia known as the *[Schutzkorps](/wiki/Schutzkorps "Schutzkorps")* was established, and carried out the persecution of Serbs.[[40]](#cite_note-Kröll2008-43)[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETomasevich2001485-44)[[42]](#cite_note-Schindler2007-45)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelikonja2003141-46)

### July Crisis

Main article: [July Crisis](/wiki/July_Crisis "July Crisis")

See also: [German entry into World War I](/wiki/German_entry_into_World_War_I "German entry into World War I"), [Austro-Hungarian entry into World War I](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_entry_into_World_War_I "Austro-Hungarian entry into World War I"), and [Russian entry into World War I](/wiki/Russian_entry_into_World_War_I "Russian entry into World War I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/The_War_of_the_Nations_WW1_337.jpg/250px-The_War_of_the_Nations_WW1_337.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_War_of_the_Nations_WW1_337.jpg)

Cheering crowds in London and Paris on the day war was declared.

The assassination initiated the July Crisis, a month of diplomatic manoeuvring among Austria-Hungary, Germany, Russia, France and Britain. Believing that Serbian intelligence helped organise Franz Ferdinand's murder, Austrian officials wanted to use the opportunity to end Serbian interference in Bosnia and saw war as the best way of achieving this.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStevenson199612-47) However, the [Foreign Ministry](/wiki/Foreign_Ministry_of_Austria-Hungary "Foreign Ministry of Austria-Hungary") had no solid proof of Serbian involvement.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMillan2013532-48) On 23 July, Austria delivered an [ultimatum](/wiki/July_Ultimatum "July Ultimatum") to Serbia, listing ten demands made intentionally unacceptable to provide an excuse for starting hostilities.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWillmott200327-49)

Serbia ordered general [mobilisation](/wiki/Mobilization "Mobilization") on 25 July, but accepted all the terms, except for those empowering Austrian representatives to suppress "subversive elements" inside Serbia, and take part in the investigation and trial of Serbians linked to the assassination.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFromkin2004196–197-50)[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMillan2013536-51) Claiming this amounted to rejection, Austria broke off diplomatic relations and ordered partial mobilisation the next day; on 28 July, they declared war on Serbia and began shelling [Belgrade](/wiki/Belgrade "Belgrade"). Russia ordered general mobilisation in support of Serbia on 30 July.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELieven2016326-52)

Anxious to ensure backing from the [SPD](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") political opposition by presenting Russia as the aggressor, German Chancellor Bethmann Hollweg delayed the commencement of war preparations until 31 July.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark2013526–527-53) That afternoon, the Russian government were handed a note requiring them to "cease all war measures against Germany and Austria-Hungary" within 12 hours.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartel2014335-54) A further German demand for neutrality was refused by the French who ordered [general mobilisation](/wiki/1914_French_mobilization "1914 French mobilization") but delayed declaring war.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert199427-55) The [German General Staff](/wiki/German_General_Staff "German General Staff") had long assumed they faced a war on two fronts; the [Schlieffen Plan](/wiki/Schlieffen_Plan "Schlieffen Plan") envisaged using 80% of the army to defeat France, then switching to Russia. Since this required them to move quickly, mobilisation orders were issued that afternoon.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClayton200345-56) Once the German ultimatum to Russia expired on the morning of 1 August, the two countries were at war.

At a meeting on 29 July, the British cabinet had narrowly decided its obligations to Belgium under the 1839 [Treaty of London](/wiki/Treaty_of_London_(1839) "Treaty of London (1839)") did not require it to oppose a German invasion with military force; however, Prime Minister [Asquith](/wiki/H._H._Asquith "H. H. Asquith") and his senior Cabinet ministers were already committed to supporting France, the Royal Navy had been mobilised, and public opinion was strongly in favour of intervention.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark2013539–541-57) On 31 July, Britain sent notes to Germany and France, asking them to respect Belgian neutrality; France pledged to do so, but Germany did not reply.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert199429-58) Aware of German plans to attack through Belgium, French Commander-in-Chief [Joseph Joffre](/wiki/Joseph_Joffre "Joseph Joffre") asked his government for permission to cross the border and pre-empt such a move. To avoid violating Belgian neutrality, he was told any advance could come only after a German invasion.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMillan2013579–580,_585-59) Instead, the French cabinet ordered its Army to withdraw 10 km behind the German frontier, to avoid provoking war. On 2 August, [Germany occupied Luxembourg](/wiki/German_occupation_of_Luxembourg_during_World_War_I "German occupation of Luxembourg during World War I") and exchanged fire with French units when German patrols entered French territory; on 3 August, they declared war on France and demanded free passage across Belgium, which was refused. Early on the morning of 4 August, the Germans invaded, and [Albert I of Belgium](/wiki/Albert_I_of_Belgium "Albert I of Belgium") called for assistance under the [Treaty of London](/wiki/Treaty_of_London_(1839) "Treaty of London (1839)").[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrowe20014–5-60)[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWillmott200329-61) Britain sent Germany an ultimatum demanding they withdraw from Belgium; when this expired at midnight, without a response, the two empires were at war.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark2013550–551-62)

## Progress of the war

Further information: [Diplomatic history of World War I](/wiki/Diplomatic_history_of_World_War_I "Diplomatic history of World War I")

### Opening hostilities

#### Confusion among the Central Powers

Germany promised to support Austria-Hungary's invasion of Serbia, but interpretations of what this meant differed. Previously tested deployment plans had been replaced early in 1914, but those had never been tested in exercises. Austro-Hungarian leaders believed Germany would cover its northern flank against Russia.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrachan2003292–296,_343–354-63)

#### Serbian campaign

Main article: [Serbian campaign](/wiki/Serbian_campaign "Serbian campaign")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/FirstSerbianArmedPlane1915.jpg/250px-FirstSerbianArmedPlane1915.jpg)](/wiki/File:FirstSerbianArmedPlane1915.jpg)

Serbian Army [Blériot XI](/wiki/Bl%C3%A9riot_XI "Blériot XI") "Oluj", 1915

Beginning on 12 August, the Austrians and Serbs clashed at the battles of the [Cer](/wiki/Battle_of_Cer "Battle of Cer") and [Kolubara](/wiki/Battle_of_Kolubara "Battle of Kolubara"); over the next two weeks, Austrian attacks were repulsed with heavy losses. As a result, Austria had to keep sizeable forces on the Serbian front, weakening their efforts against Russia.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts2005172-64) Serbia's victory against Austria-Hungary in the 1914 invasion has been called one of the major upset victories of the twentieth century.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchindler2002159–195-65) In 1915, the campaign saw the first use of [anti-aircraft warfare](/wiki/Anti-aircraft_warfare "Anti-aircraft warfare") after an Austrian plane was shot down with [ground-to-air](/wiki/Surface-to-air_missile "Surface-to-air missile") fire, as well as the first [medical evacuation](/wiki/Medical_evacuation "Medical evacuation") by the Serbian army.[[63]](#cite_note-66)[[64]](#cite_note-67)

#### German offensive in Belgium and France

Main article: [Great Retreat](/wiki/Great_Retreat "Great Retreat")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/German_soldiers_in_a_railroad_car_on_the_way_to_the_front_during_early_World_War_I%2C_taken_in_1914._Taken_from_greatwar.nl_site.jpg/250px-German_soldiers_in_a_railroad_car_on_the_way_to_the_front_during_early_World_War_I%2C_taken_in_1914._Taken_from_greatwar.nl_site.jpg)](/wiki/File:German_soldiers_in_a_railroad_car_on_the_way_to_the_front_during_early_World_War_I,_taken_in_1914._Taken_from_greatwar.nl_site.jpg)

German soldiers on the way to the front in 1914; at this stage, all sides expected the conflict to be a short one.

Upon mobilisation, in accordance with the [Schlieffen Plan](/wiki/Schlieffen_Plan "Schlieffen Plan"), 80% of the [German Army](/wiki/German_Army_order_of_battle_(1914) "German Army order of battle (1914)") was located on the Western Front, with the remainder acting as a screening force in the East. Rather than a direct attack across their shared frontier, the German right wing would sweep through the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") and [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), then swing south, encircling Paris and trapping the French army against the Swiss border. The plan's creator, [Alfred von Schlieffen](/wiki/Alfred_von_Schlieffen "Alfred von Schlieffen"), head of the [German General Staff](/wiki/German_General_Staff "German General Staff") from 1891 to 1906, estimated that this would take six weeks, after which the German army would transfer to the East and defeat the Russians.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStevenson200422-68)

The plan was substantially modified by his successor, [Helmuth von Moltke the Younger](/wiki/Helmuth_von_Moltke_the_Younger "Helmuth von Moltke the Younger"). Under Schlieffen, 85% of German forces in the west were assigned to the right wing, with the remainder holding along the frontier. By keeping his left-wing deliberately weak, he hoped to lure the French into an offensive into the "lost provinces" of [Alsace-Lorraine](/wiki/Alsace-Lorraine "Alsace-Lorraine"), which was the strategy envisaged by their [Plan XVII](/wiki/Plan_XVII "Plan XVII").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStevenson200422-68) However, Moltke grew concerned that the French might push too hard on his left flank and as the German Army increased in size from 1908 to 1914, he changed the allocation of forces between the two wings to 70:30.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHorne196422-69) He also considered Dutch neutrality essential for German trade and cancelled the incursion into the Netherlands, which meant any delays in Belgium threatened the viability of the plan.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStevenson200423-70) Historian [Richard Holmes](/wiki/Richard_Holmes_(military_historian) "Richard Holmes (military historian)") argues that these changes meant the right wing was not strong enough to achieve decisive success.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHolmes2014194,_211-71)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Georges_Scott%2C_A_la_ba%C3%AFonnette_%21.jpg/250px-Georges_Scott%2C_A_la_ba%C3%AFonnette_%21.jpg)](/wiki/File:Georges_Scott,_A_la_ba%C3%AFonnette_!.jpg)

French bayonet charge during the [Battle of the Frontiers](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Frontiers "Battle of the Frontiers"); by the end of August, French casualties exceeded 260,000, including 75,000 dead.

The initial German advance in the West was very successful. By the end of August, the Allied left, which included the [British Expeditionary Force](/wiki/British_Expeditionary_Force_(World_War_I) "British Expeditionary Force (World War I)") (BEF), was in [full retreat](/wiki/Great_Retreat "Great Retreat"), and the French offensive in Alsace-Lorraine was a disastrous failure, with casualties exceeding 260,000.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStevenson201254-72) German planning provided broad strategic instructions while allowing army commanders considerable freedom in carrying them out at the front, but [Alexander von Kluck](/wiki/Alexander_von_Kluck "Alexander von Kluck") used this freedom to disobey orders, opening a gap between the German armies as they closed on Paris.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJackson201855-73) The French army, reinforced by the British expeditionary corps, seized this opportunity to counter-attack and pushed the German army 40 to 80 km back. Both armies were then so exhausted that no decisive move could be implemented, so they settled in trenches, with the vain hope of breaking through as soon as they could build local superiority.

In 1911, the Russian [Stavka](/wiki/Stavka "Stavka") agreed with the French to attack Germany within fifteen days of mobilisation, ten days before the Germans had anticipated, although it meant the two Russian armies that entered [East Prussia](/wiki/East_Prussia "East Prussia") on 17 August did so without many of their support elements.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELieven2016327-74)

By the end of 1914, German troops held strong defensive positions inside France, controlled the bulk of France's domestic coalfields, and inflicted 230,000 more casualties than it lost itself. However, communications problems and questionable command decisions cost Germany the chance of a decisive outcome, while it had failed to achieve the primary objective of avoiding a long, two-front war.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts2005376–378-75) As was apparent to several German leaders, this amounted to a strategic defeat; shortly after the [First Battle of the Marne](/wiki/First_Battle_of_the_Marne "First Battle of the Marne"), [Crown Prince Wilhelm](/wiki/Wilhelm,_German_Crown_Prince "Wilhelm, German Crown Prince") told an American reporter "We have lost the war. It will go on for a long time but lost it is already."[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHorne1964221-76)

#### Asia and the Pacific

Main article: [Asian and Pacific theatre of World War I](/wiki/Asian_and_Pacific_theatre_of_World_War_I "Asian and Pacific theatre of World War I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Siege_of_Tsingtao%2C_soldiers_of_IJA_18th_division_took_over_german_trench_Kopie.jpg/250px-Siege_of_Tsingtao%2C_soldiers_of_IJA_18th_division_took_over_german_trench_Kopie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Siege_of_Tsingtao,_soldiers_of_IJA_18th_division_took_over_german_trench_Kopie.jpg)

Japanese soldiers occupy a captured German trench during the [Siege of Tsingtao](/wiki/Siege_of_Tsingtao "Siege of Tsingtao"), 1914

On 30 August 1914, New Zealand [occupied German Samoa](/wiki/Occupation_of_German_Samoa "Occupation of German Samoa") (now [Samoa](/wiki/Samoa "Samoa")). On 11 September, the [Australian Naval and Military Expeditionary Force](/wiki/Australian_Naval_and_Military_Expeditionary_Force "Australian Naval and Military Expeditionary Force") landed on the island of [New Britain](/wiki/New_Britain "New Britain"), then part of [German New Guinea](/wiki/German_New_Guinea "German New Guinea"). On 28 October, the German cruiser [SMS *Emden*](/wiki/SMS_Emden "SMS Emden") sank the [Russian cruiser *Zhemchug*](/wiki/Russian_cruiser_Zhemchug "Russian cruiser Zhemchug") in the [Battle of Penang](/wiki/Battle_of_Penang "Battle of Penang"). Japan declared war on Germany before seizing territories in the Pacific, which later became the [South Seas Mandate](/wiki/South_Seas_Mandate "South Seas Mandate"), as well as German [Treaty ports](/wiki/Treaty_ports "Treaty ports") on the Chinese [Shandong](/wiki/Shandong "Shandong") peninsula at [Tsingtao](/wiki/Siege_of_Tsingtao "Siege of Tsingtao"). After Vienna refused to withdraw its cruiser [SMS *Kaiserin Elisabeth*](/wiki/SMS_Kaiserin_Elisabeth "SMS Kaiserin Elisabeth") from Tsingtao, Japan declared war on Austria-Hungary, and the ship was scuttled in November 1914.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonko201279-77) Within a few months, Allied forces had seized all German territories in the Pacific, leaving only isolated commerce raiders and a few holdouts in [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea").[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeegan1998224–232-78)[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFalls196079–80-79)

#### African campaigns

Main article: [African theatre of World War I](/wiki/African_theatre_of_World_War_I "African theatre of World War I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/12pdr8cwtFortDachangCameroons1915.jpg/250px-12pdr8cwtFortDachangCameroons1915.jpg)](/wiki/File:12pdr8cwtFortDachangCameroons1915.jpg)

British artillery in Kamerun, 1915

Some of the first clashes of the war involved British, French, and German colonial forces in Africa. On 6–7 August, French and British troops invaded the German protectorates of [Togoland](/wiki/Togoland "Togoland") and [Kamerun](/wiki/Kamerun "Kamerun"). On 10 August, German forces in [South-West Africa](/wiki/German_South_West_Africa "German South West Africa") attacked South Africa; sporadic and fierce fighting continued for the rest of the war. The German colonial forces in [German East Africa](/wiki/German_East_Africa "German East Africa"), led by Colonel [Paul von Lettow-Vorbeck](/wiki/Paul_von_Lettow-Vorbeck "Paul von Lettow-Vorbeck"), fought a [guerrilla warfare](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare") campaign and only surrendered two weeks after the armistice took effect in Europe.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarwell1989353-80)

#### Indian support for the Allies

Main article: [Indian Army during World War I](/wiki/Indian_Army_during_World_War_I "Indian Army during World War I")

Further information: [Hindu–German Conspiracy](/wiki/Hindu%E2%80%93German_Conspiracy "Hindu–German Conspiracy"), [Niedermayer–Hentig Expedition](/wiki/Niedermayer%E2%80%93Hentig_Expedition "Niedermayer–Hentig Expedition"), and [Third Anglo-Afghan War](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Afghan_War "Third Anglo-Afghan War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Indian_forces_on_their_way_to_the_Front_in_Flanders_-_first_world_war_2.jpg/250px-Indian_forces_on_their_way_to_the_Front_in_Flanders_-_first_world_war_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indian_forces_on_their_way_to_the_Front_in_Flanders_-_first_world_war_2.jpg)

[British Indian Army](/wiki/British_Indian_Army "British Indian Army") infantry divisions in France; these troops were withdrawn in December 1915, and served in the [Mesopotamian campaign](/wiki/Mesopotamian_campaign "Mesopotamian campaign").

Before the war, Germany had attempted to use Indian nationalism and pan-Islamism to its advantage, a policy continued post-1914 by [instigating uprisings in India](/wiki/Hindu%E2%80%93German_Conspiracy "Hindu–German Conspiracy"), while the [Niedermayer–Hentig Expedition](/wiki/Niedermayer%E2%80%93Hentig_Expedition "Niedermayer–Hentig Expedition") urged Afghanistan to join the war on the side of Central Powers. However, contrary to British fears of a revolt in India, the outbreak of the war saw a reduction in nationalist activity.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown1994197–198-81)[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown1994201–203-82) Leaders from the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") and other groups believed support for the British war effort would hasten [Indian Home Rule](/wiki/Indian_Home_Rule_movement "Indian Home Rule movement"), a promise allegedly made explicit in 1917 by [Edwin Montagu](/wiki/Edwin_Montagu "Edwin Montagu"), the [Secretary of State for India](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_India "Secretary of State for India").[[80]](#cite_note-83)

In 1914, the [British Indian Army](/wiki/British_Indian_Army "British Indian Army") was larger than the British Army itself, and between 1914 and 1918 an estimated 1.3 million Indian soldiers and labourers served in Europe, Africa, and the Middle East. In all, 140,000 soldiers served on the Western Front and nearly 700,000 in the Middle East, with 47,746 killed and 65,126 wounded.[[81]](#cite_note-84) The suffering engendered by the war, as well as the failure of the British government to grant self-government to India afterward, bred disillusionment, resulting in [the campaign for full independence](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement") led by [Mahatma Gandhi](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi "Mahatma Gandhi").[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHorniman198445-85)

### Western Front

Main article: [Western Front (World War I)](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)")

#### Trench warfare begins

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Indian_infantry_digging_trenches_Fauquissart%2C_France_%28Photo_24-299%29.jpg/250px-Indian_infantry_digging_trenches_Fauquissart%2C_France_%28Photo_24-299%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indian_infantry_digging_trenches_Fauquissart,_France_(Photo_24-299).jpg)

[British Indian soldiers](/wiki/Indian_Army_during_World_War_I "Indian Army during World War I") digging trenches in [Laventie](/wiki/Laventie "Laventie"), France, 1915

Pre-war military tactics that had emphasised open warfare and individual riflemen proved obsolete when confronted with conditions prevailing in 1914. Barbed wire, machine guns and artillery allowed the creation of strong defensive systems that made massed infantry advances extremely difficult.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERaudzens1990424-86) Both sides struggled to develop tactics for breaching entrenched positions without heavy casualties. In time, the [tank](/wiki/Tanks_in_World_War_I "Tanks in World War I") helped to make the front more mobile.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERaudzens1990421–423-87)

After the [First Battle of the Marne](/wiki/First_Battle_of_the_Marne "First Battle of the Marne") in September 1914, Allied and German forces unsuccessfully tried to outflank each other, a series of manoeuvres later known as the "[Race to the Sea](/wiki/Race_to_the_Sea "Race to the Sea")". By the end of 1914, the opposing forces confronted each other along an uninterrupted line of entrenched positions from the [Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel") to the Swiss border.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert199499-88) Since the Germans were normally able to choose where to stand, they generally held the high ground, while their trenches tended to be better built; those constructed by the French and English were initially considered "temporary", only needed until an offensive would destroy the German defences.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodspeed1985199-89) Both sides tried to break the stalemate using scientific and technological advances. On 22 April 1915, at the [Second Battle of Ypres](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Ypres "Second Battle of Ypres"), the Germans (violating the [Hague Convention](/wiki/Hague_Conventions_of_1899_and_1907 "Hague Conventions of 1899 and 1907")) used [chlorine](/wiki/Chlorine "Chlorine") gas for the first time on the Western Front.[[87]](#cite_note-:0-90)[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELove1996-91)

#### Continuation of trench warfare

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/The_Battle_of_the_Somme%2C_July-november_1916_Q4218.jpg/250px-The_Battle_of_the_Somme%2C_July-november_1916_Q4218.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Battle_of_the_Somme,_July-november_1916_Q4218.jpg)

German casualties at the Somme, 1916

In February 1916, the Germans attacked French defensive positions at the [Battle of Verdun](/wiki/Battle_of_Verdun "Battle of Verdun"), lasting until December 1916. Casualties were greater for the French, but the Germans bled heavily as well, with anywhere from 700,000[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDupuy19931042-92) to 975,000[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrant2005276-93) casualties between the two combatants. Verdun became a symbol of French determination and self-sacrifice.[[91]](#cite_note-94)

The [Battle of the Somme](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Somme "Battle of the Somme") was an Anglo-French offensive from July to November 1916. The [opening day](/wiki/First_day_on_the_Somme "First day on the Somme"), on 1 July 1916, was the bloodiest single day in the history of the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army"), which suffered 57,500 casualties, including 19,200 dead. As a whole, the Somme offensive led to an estimated 420,000 British casualties, along with 200,000 French and 500,000 Germans.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2008271-95) The diseases that emerged in the trenches were a major killer on both sides. The living conditions led to disease and infection, such as [trench foot](/wiki/Trench_foot "Trench foot"), [lice](/wiki/Louse "Louse"), [typhus](/wiki/Typhus "Typhus"), [trench fever](/wiki/Trench_fever "Trench fever"), and the '[Spanish flu](/wiki/Spanish_flu "Spanish flu")'.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChorba20182136–2137-96)

### Naval war

Main article: [Naval warfare of World War I](/wiki/Naval_warfare_of_World_War_I "Naval warfare of World War I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Hochseeflotte_2.jpg/250px-Hochseeflotte_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hochseeflotte_2.jpg)

Battleships of the [High Seas Fleet](/wiki/High_Seas_Fleet "High Seas Fleet"), 1917

At the start of the war, German [cruisers](/wiki/Cruiser "Cruiser") were scattered across the globe, some of which were subsequently used to attack Allied [merchant shipping](/wiki/Merchant_shipping "Merchant shipping"). These were systematically hunted down by the Royal Navy, though not before causing considerable damage. One of the most successful was the [SMS *Emden*](/wiki/SMS_Emden "SMS Emden"), part of the German [East Asia Squadron](/wiki/East_Asia_Squadron "East Asia Squadron") stationed at [Qingdao](/wiki/Qingdao "Qingdao"), which seized or sank 15 merchantmen, a Russian cruiser and a French destroyer. Most of the squadron was returning to Germany when it sank two British armoured cruisers at the [Battle of Coronel](/wiki/Battle_of_Coronel "Battle of Coronel") in November 1914, before being virtually destroyed at the [Battle of the Falkland Islands](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Falkland_Islands "Battle of the Falkland Islands") in December. The [SMS *Dresden*](/wiki/SMS_Dresden_(1907) "SMS Dresden (1907)") escaped with a few auxiliaries, but after the [Battle of Más a Tierra](/wiki/Battle_of_M%C3%A1s_a_Tierra "Battle of Más a Tierra"), these too were either destroyed or interned.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor200739–47-97)

Soon after the outbreak of hostilities, Britain began a naval [blockade of Germany](/wiki/Blockade_of_Germany_(1914%E2%80%931919) "Blockade of Germany (1914–1919)"). This proved effective in cutting off vital supplies, though it violated accepted international law.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeene20065-98) Britain also mined international waters which closed off entire sections of the ocean, even to neutral ships.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalpern1995293-99) Since there was limited response to this tactic, Germany expected a similar response to its unrestricted submarine warfare.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZieger200150-100)

The [Battle of Jutland](/wiki/Battle_of_Jutland "Battle of Jutland")[[d]](#cite_note-101) in May/June 1916 was the only full-scale clash of battleships during the war, and one of the largest in history. The clash was indecisive, though the Germans inflicted more damage than they received; thereafter the bulk of the German [High Seas Fleet](/wiki/High_Seas_Fleet "High Seas Fleet") was confined to port.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlack201616–21-102)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/NationaalArchief_uboat155London.jpg/250px-NationaalArchief_uboat155London.jpg)](/wiki/File:NationaalArchief_uboat155London.jpg)

[*U-155*](/wiki/German_submarine_Deutschland "German submarine Deutschland") exhibited near Tower Bridge in London, after the 1918 Armistice

German [U-boats](/wiki/U-boat "U-boat") attempted to cut the supply lines between North America and Britain.[[99]](#cite_note-Sheffield-103) The nature of [submarine warfare](/wiki/Submarine_warfare "Submarine warfare") meant that attacks often came without warning, giving the crews of the merchant ships little hope of survival.[[99]](#cite_note-Sheffield-103)[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert1994306-104) The United States launched a protest, and Germany changed its rules of engagement. After [the sinking](/wiki/Sinking_of_the_RMS_Lusitania "Sinking of the RMS Lusitania") of the passenger ship [RMS *Lusitania*](/wiki/RMS_Lusitania "RMS Lusitania") in 1915, Germany promised not to target passenger liners, while Britain armed its merchant ships, placing them beyond the protection of the "[cruiser rules](/wiki/Cruiser_rules "Cruiser rules")", which demanded warning and movement of crews to "a place of safety" (a standard that lifeboats did not meet).[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_der_Porten1969-105) Finally, in early 1917, Germany adopted a policy of [unrestricted submarine warfare](/wiki/Unrestricted_submarine_warfare "Unrestricted submarine warfare"), realising the Americans would eventually enter the war.[[99]](#cite_note-Sheffield-103)[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones200180-106) Germany sought to strangle Allied [sea lanes](/wiki/Sea_lane "Sea lane") before the United States could transport a large army overseas, but, after initial successes, eventually failed to do so.[[99]](#cite_note-Sheffield-103)

The U-boat threat lessened in 1917, when merchant ships began travelling in [convoys](/wiki/Convoys_in_World_War_I "Convoys in World War I"), escorted by [destroyers](/wiki/Destroyer "Destroyer"). This tactic made it difficult for U-boats to find targets, which significantly lessened losses; after the [hydrophone](/wiki/Hydrophone "Hydrophone") and [depth charges](/wiki/Depth_charge "Depth charge") were introduced, destroyers could potentially successfully attack a submerged submarine. Convoys slowed the flow of supplies since ships had to wait as convoys were assembled; the solution was an extensive program of building new freighters. Troopships were too fast for the submarines and did not travel the North Atlantic in convoys.[[103]](#cite_note-107) The U-boats sunk more than 5,000 Allied ships, at the cost of 199 submarines.[[104]](#cite_note-108)

World War I also saw the first use of [aircraft carriers](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier") in combat, with [HMS *Furious*](/wiki/HMS_Furious_(47) "HMS Furious (47)") launching [Sopwith Camels](/wiki/Sopwith_Camel "Sopwith Camel") in a successful raid against the [Zeppelin](/wiki/Zeppelin "Zeppelin") hangars at [Tondern](/wiki/Tondern_raid "Tondern raid") in July 1918, as well as [blimps](/wiki/Blimp "Blimp") for antisubmarine patrol.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrice1980-109)

### Southern theatres

#### War in the Balkans

Main articles: [Balkan Front (World War I)](/wiki/Balkan_Front_(World_War_I) "Balkan Front (World War I)"), [Bulgaria during World War I](/wiki/Bulgaria_during_World_War_I "Bulgaria during World War I"), [Serbian campaign](/wiki/Serbian_campaign "Serbian campaign"), and [Macedonian front](/wiki/Macedonian_front "Macedonian front")

See also: [Noemvriana](/wiki/Noemvriana "Noemvriana")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Fl%C3%BCchtlingstransport_Leibnitz_-_k.k._Innenministerium_-_1914.jpg/250px-Fl%C3%BCchtlingstransport_Leibnitz_-_k.k._Innenministerium_-_1914.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fl%C3%BCchtlingstransport_Leibnitz_-_k.k._Innenministerium_-_1914.jpg)

Refugee transport from Serbia in [Leibnitz](/wiki/Leibnitz "Leibnitz"), [Styria](/wiki/Styria "Styria"), 1914

Faced with Russia in the east, Austria-Hungary could spare only one-third of its army to attack Serbia. After suffering heavy losses, the Austrians briefly occupied the Serbian capital, [Belgrade](/wiki/Belgrade "Belgrade"). A Serbian counter-attack in the Battle of Kolubara succeeded in driving them from the country by the end of 1914. For the first 10 months of 1915, Austria-Hungary used most of its military reserves to fight Italy. German and Austro-Hungarian diplomats scored a coup by persuading Bulgaria to join the attack on Serbia.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts2005[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid2YqjfHLyyj8C_241–]-110) The Austro-Hungarian provinces of [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia"), Croatia and [Bosnia](/wiki/Bosnia_(region) "Bosnia (region)") provided troops for Austria-Hungary. Montenegro allied itself with Serbia.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeiberg200554–55-111)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Bulgaria_southern_front.jpg/250px-Bulgaria_southern_front.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bulgaria_southern_front.jpg)

Bulgarian soldiers in a trench, preparing to fire against an incoming aeroplane

Bulgaria declared war on Serbia on 14 October 1915, and joined in the attack by the Austro-Hungarian army under Mackensen's army of 250,000 that was already underway. Serbia was conquered in a little more than a month, as the Central Powers, now including Bulgaria, sent in 600,000 troops in total. The Serbian army, fighting on two fronts and facing certain defeat, retreated into northern [Albania](/wiki/Principality_of_Albania "Principality of Albania"). The Serbs suffered defeat in the [Battle of Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo_offensive_(1915) "Kosovo offensive (1915)"). Montenegro covered the Serbian retreat toward the Adriatic coast in the [Battle of Mojkovac](/wiki/Battle_of_Mojkovac "Battle of Mojkovac") on 6–7 January 1916, but ultimately the Austrians also conquered Montenegro. The surviving Serbian soldiers were evacuated to Greece.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts20051075–1076-112) After the conquest, Serbia was divided between Austro-Hungary and Bulgaria.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDiNardo2015102-113)

In late 1915, a Franco-British force landed at [Salonica](/wiki/Thessaloniki "Thessaloniki") in Greece to offer assistance and to pressure its government to declare war against the Central Powers. However, the pro-German [King Constantine I](/wiki/Constantine_I_of_Greece "Constantine I of Greece") dismissed the pro-Allied government of [Eleftherios Venizelos](/wiki/Eleftherios_Venizelos "Eleftherios Venizelos") before the Allied expeditionary force arrived.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeiberg2005108–110-114)

The Macedonian front was at first mostly static. French and Serbian forces retook limited areas of Macedonia by recapturing [Bitola](/wiki/Bitola "Bitola") on 19 November 1916, following the costly [Monastir offensive](/wiki/Monastir_offensive "Monastir offensive"), which brought stabilisation of the front.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHall201011-115)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Austrians_executing_Serbs_1917.JPG/250px-Austrians_executing_Serbs_1917.JPG)](/wiki/File:Austrians_executing_Serbs_1917.JPG)

Austro-Hungarian troops executing captured Serbians, 1917. [Serbia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbia "Kingdom of Serbia") lost about 850,000 people during the war, a quarter of its pre-war population.[[112]](#cite_note-116)

Serbian and French troops finally made a breakthrough in September 1918 in the [Vardar offensive](/wiki/Vardar_offensive "Vardar offensive"), after most German and Austro-Hungarian troops had been withdrawn. The Bulgarians were defeated at the [Battle of Dobro Pole](/wiki/Battle_of_Dobro_Pole "Battle of Dobro Pole"), and by 25 September British and French troops had crossed the border into Bulgaria proper as the Bulgarian army collapsed. Bulgaria capitulated four days later, on 29 September 1918.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerWoodMurphy1999150–152-117) The German high command responded by despatching troops to hold the line, but these forces were too weak to re-establish a front.[[114]](#cite_note-militera-118)

The Allied breakthrough on the Macedonian front cut communications between the Ottoman Empire and the other [Central Powers](/wiki/Central_Powers "Central Powers"), and made Vienna vulnerable to attack. Hindenburg and Ludendorff concluded that the strategic and operational balance had now shifted decidedly against the [Central Powers](/wiki/Central_Powers "Central Powers") and, a day after the Bulgarian collapse, insisted on an immediate peace settlement.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoughty2005491-119)

#### Ottoman Empire

Main article: [Ottoman Empire in World War I](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire_in_World_War_I "Ottoman Empire in World War I")

See also: [Middle Eastern theatre of World War I](/wiki/Middle_Eastern_theatre_of_World_War_I "Middle Eastern theatre of World War I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Scene_just_before_the_evacuation_at_Anzac._Australian_troops_charging_near_a_Turkish_trench._When_they_got_there_the..._-_NARA_-_533108.jpg/250px-Scene_just_before_the_evacuation_at_Anzac._Australian_troops_charging_near_a_Turkish_trench._When_they_got_there_the..._-_NARA_-_533108.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scene_just_before_the_evacuation_at_Anzac._Australian_troops_charging_near_a_Turkish_trench._When_they_got_there_the..._-_NARA_-_533108.jpg)

Australian troops charging near a Turkish trench during the [Gallipoli campaign](/wiki/Gallipoli_campaign "Gallipoli campaign")

The Ottomans threatened Russia's [Caucasian](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus") territories and Britain's communications with India via the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal"). The Ottoman Empire took advantage of the European powers' preoccupation with the war and conducted large-scale ethnic cleansing of the [Armenian](/wiki/Armenians "Armenians"), [Greek](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks"), and [Assyrian](/wiki/Assyrian_people "Assyrian people") Christian populations—the [Armenian genocide](/wiki/Armenian_genocide "Armenian genocide"), [Greek genocide](/wiki/Greek_genocide "Greek genocide"), and [Assyrian genocide](/wiki/Assyrian_genocide "Assyrian genocide") respectively.[[116]](#cite_note-120)[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJanuary200714-121)
[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELieberman201380–81-122)

The British and French opened overseas fronts with the [Gallipoli](/wiki/Gallipoli_campaign "Gallipoli campaign") (1915) and [Mesopotamian campaigns](/wiki/Mesopotamian_campaign "Mesopotamian campaign") (1914). In Gallipoli, the Ottoman Empire successfully repelled the British, French, and [Australian and New Zealand Army Corps](/wiki/Australian_and_New_Zealand_Army_Corps "Australian and New Zealand Army Corps") (ANZACs). In [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), by contrast, after the defeat of the British defenders in the [siege of Kut](/wiki/Siege_of_Kut "Siege of Kut") by the Ottomans (1915–1916), British Imperial forces reorganised and captured [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad") in March 1917. The British were aided in Mesopotamia by local Arab and Assyrian fighters, while the Ottomans employed local [Kurdish](/wiki/Kurds "Kurds") and [Turcoman](/wiki/Iraqi_Turkmen "Iraqi Turkmen") tribes.[[119]](#cite_note-123)

The [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal") was defended from Ottoman attacks in 1915 and 1916; in August 1916, a German and Ottoman force was defeated at the [Battle of Romani](/wiki/Battle_of_Romani "Battle of Romani") by the [ANZAC Mounted Division](/wiki/ANZAC_Mounted_Division "ANZAC Mounted Division") and the [52nd (Lowland) Infantry Division](/wiki/52nd_(Lowland)_Infantry_Division "52nd (Lowland) Infantry Division"). Following this victory, an [Egyptian Expeditionary Force](/wiki/Egyptian_Expeditionary_Force "Egyptian Expeditionary Force") advanced across the [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula"), pushing Ottoman forces back in the [Battle of Magdhaba](/wiki/Battle_of_Magdhaba "Battle of Magdhaba") in December and the [Battle of Rafa](/wiki/Battle_of_Rafa "Battle of Rafa") on the border between the Egyptian [Sinai](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula") and Ottoman Palestine in January 1917.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrawfordMcGibbon2007219–220-124)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Sarikam.jpg/250px-Sarikam.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sarikam.jpg)

Russian forest trench at the [Battle of Sarikamish](/wiki/Battle_of_Sarikamish "Battle of Sarikamish"), 1914–1915

Russian armies generally had success in the [Caucasus campaign](/wiki/Caucasus_campaign "Caucasus campaign"). [Enver Pasha](/wiki/Enver_Pasha "Enver Pasha"), supreme commander of the Ottoman armed forces, dreamed of re-conquering central Asia and areas that had been previously lost to Russia. He was, however, a poor commander.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFromkin2004119-125) He launched an offensive against the Russians in the Caucasus in December 1914 with 100,000 troops, insisting on a frontal attack against mountainous Russian positions in winter. He lost 86% of his force at the [Battle of Sarikamish](/wiki/Battle_of_Sarikamish "Battle of Sarikamish").[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinterhoff1984499–503-126) General [Nikolai Yudenich](/wiki/Nikolai_Yudenich "Nikolai Yudenich"), the Russian commander from 1915 to 1916, drove the Turks out of most of the southern [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus").[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinterhoff1984499–503-126)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Ottoman_15th_Corps.jpg/250px-Ottoman_15th_Corps.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ottoman_15th_Corps.jpg)

Kaiser Wilhelm II and [Prince Leopold of Bavaria](/wiki/Prince_Leopold_of_Bavaria "Prince Leopold of Bavaria") inspecting Turkish troops of the 15th Corps in East Galicia, Austria-Hungary (now Poland)

The Ottoman Empire, with German support, invaded Persia (modern [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran")) in December 1914 to cut off British and Russian access to [petroleum reservoirs](/wiki/Petroleum_reservoir "Petroleum reservoir") around [Baku](/wiki/Baku "Baku").[[123]](#cite_note-127) Persia, ostensibly neutral, had long been under British and Russian influence. The Ottomans and Germans were aided by [Kurdish](/wiki/Kurds "Kurds") and [Azeri](/wiki/Azerbaijanis "Azerbaijanis") forces, together with a large number of major Iranian tribes, while the Russians and British had the support of Armenian and [Assyrian forces](/wiki/Assyrian_volunteers "Assyrian volunteers"). The [Persian campaign](/wiki/Persian_campaign_(World_War_I) "Persian campaign (World War I)") lasted until 1918 and ended in failure for the Ottomans and their allies. However, the Russian withdrawal from the war in 1917 led Armenian and Assyrian forces to be cut off from supply lines, outnumbered, outgunned and isolated, forcing them to fight and flee towards British lines in northern Mesopotamia.[[124]](#cite_note-128)

The [Arab Revolt](/wiki/Arab_Revolt "Arab Revolt"), instigated by the British [Foreign Office](/wiki/Foreign,_Commonwealth_and_Development_Office "Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office"), started in June 1916 with the [Battle of Mecca](/wiki/Battle_of_Mecca_(1916) "Battle of Mecca (1916)"), led by [Sharif Hussein](/wiki/Hussein_bin_Ali,_Sharif_of_Mecca "Hussein bin Ali, Sharif of Mecca"). The Sharif declared the independence of the [Kingdom of Hejaz](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hejaz "Kingdom of Hejaz") and, with British assistance, conquered much of Ottoman-held Arabia, resulting finally in the Ottoman surrender of Damascus. [Fakhri Pasha](/wiki/Fakhri_Pasha "Fakhri Pasha"), the Ottoman commander of [Medina](/wiki/Medina "Medina"), resisted for more than 2+1⁄2 years during the [siege of Medina](/wiki/Siege_of_Medina "Siege of Medina") before surrendering in January 1919.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESachar1970122–138-129)

The [Senussi](/wiki/Senusiyya "Senusiyya") tribe, along the border of [Italian Libya](/wiki/Italian_Libya "Italian Libya") and [British Egypt](/wiki/British_Egypt "British Egypt"), incited and armed by the Turks, waged a small-scale [guerrilla war](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare") against Allied troops. The British were forced to dispatch 12,000 troops to oppose them in the [Senussi campaign](/wiki/Senussi_campaign "Senussi campaign"). Their rebellion was finally crushed in mid-1916.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert1994-130)

Total Allied casualties on the Ottoman fronts amounted to 650,000 men. Total Ottoman casualties were 725,000, with 325,000 dead and 400,000 wounded.[[127]](#cite_note-Brief_Ottoman_History-131)

#### Italian Front

Main articles: [Italian front (World War I)](/wiki/Italian_front_(World_War_I) "Italian front (World War I)"), [White War](/wiki/White_War "White War"), and [Military history of Italy during World War I](/wiki/Military_history_of_Italy_during_World_War_I "Military history of Italy during World War I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Italian_Front_1915-1917.jpg/250px-Italian_Front_1915-1917.jpg)](/wiki/File:Italian_Front_1915-1917.jpg)

[Isonzo Offensives 1915–1917](/wiki/Battles_of_the_Isonzo "Battles of the Isonzo")

Though Italy joined the Triple Alliance in 1882, a treaty with its traditional Austrian enemy was so controversial that subsequent governments denied its existence and the terms were only made public in 1915.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThompson200913-132) This arose from [nationalist](/wiki/Italian_nationalism "Italian nationalism") designs on Austro-Hungarian territory in [Trentino](/wiki/Trentino "Trentino"), the [Austrian Littoral](/wiki/Austrian_Littoral "Austrian Littoral"), [Rijeka](/wiki/Rijeka "Rijeka") and [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia"), considered vital to secure the borders established in [1866](/wiki/Third_Italian_War_of_Independence "Third Italian War of Independence").[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThompson20099–10-133) In 1902, Rome secretly had agreed with France to remain neutral if the latter was attacked by Germany, effectively nullifying its role in the Triple Alliance.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGardner2015120-134)

When the war began in 1914, Italy argued the Triple Alliance was defensive and it was not obliged to support an Austrian attack on Serbia. Opposition to joining the Central Powers increased when Turkey became a member in September, since in [1911 Italy had occupied](/wiki/Italo-Turkish_War "Italo-Turkish War") Ottoman possessions in [Libya](/wiki/Italian_colonization_of_Libya "Italian colonization of Libya") and the [Dodecanese](/wiki/Italian_Islands_of_the_Aegean "Italian Islands of the Aegean") islands.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThompson200914-135) To secure Italian neutrality, the Central Powers offered them [Tunisia](/wiki/French_protectorate_of_Tunisia "French protectorate of Tunisia"), while in return for an immediate entry into the war, the Allies agreed to their demands for Austrian territory and sovereignty over the Dodecanese.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThompson200930–31-136) Although they remained secret, these provisions were incorporated into the April 1915 [Treaty of London](/wiki/Treaty_of_London_(1915) "Treaty of London (1915)"), and Italy joined the Allies. On 23 May, Italy declared war on Austria-Hungary,[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert1994166-137) and on Germany fifteen months later.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/1917_ortler_vorgipfelstellung_3850_m_highest_trench_in_history_of_first_world_war.jpg/250px-1917_ortler_vorgipfelstellung_3850_m_highest_trench_in_history_of_first_world_war.jpg)](/wiki/File:1917_ortler_vorgipfelstellung_3850_m_highest_trench_in_history_of_first_world_war.jpg)

Austro-Hungarian trench at 3,850 metres in the [Ortler Alps](/wiki/Ortler_Alps "Ortler Alps"), one of the most challenging fronts of the war

The pre-1914 Italian army was short of officers, trained men, adequate transport and modern weapons; by April 1915, some of these deficiencies had been remedied but it was still unprepared for the major offensive required by the Treaty of London.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThompson200957-138) The advantage of superior numbers was offset by the difficult terrain; much of the fighting took place high in the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps") and [Dolomites](/wiki/Dolomites "Dolomites"), where trench lines had to be cut through rock and ice and keeping troops supplied was a major challenge. These issues were exacerbated by unimaginative strategies and tactics.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarshallJosephy1982108-139) Between 1915 and 1917, the Italian commander, [Luigi Cadorna](/wiki/Luigi_Cadorna "Luigi Cadorna"), undertook [a series of frontal assaults along the Isonzo](/wiki/Battles_of_the_Isonzo "Battles of the Isonzo"), which made little progress and cost many lives; by the end of the war, Italian combat deaths totalled around 548,000.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFornassin201739–62-140)

In the spring of 1916, the Austro-Hungarians counterattacked in [Asiago](/wiki/Asiago "Asiago") in the *[Strafexpedition](/wiki/Battle_of_Asiago "Battle of Asiago")*, but made little progress and were pushed by the Italians back to Tyrol.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThompson2009163-141) Although Italy occupied southern [Albania](/wiki/Albania_during_World_War_I "Albania during World War I") in May 1916, their main focus was the Isonzo front which, after the [capture of Gorizia](/wiki/Battle_of_Doberd%C3%B2 "Battle of Doberdò") in August 1916, remained static until October 1917.
