After a combined Austro-German force won a major victory at [Caporetto](/wiki/Battle_of_Caporetto "Battle of Caporetto"), Cadorna was replaced by [Armando Diaz](/wiki/Armando_Diaz "Armando Diaz") who retreated more than 100 kilometres (62 mi) before holding positions along the [Piave River](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_the_Piave_River "Second Battle of the Piave River").[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert1994317-142) A second Austrian [offensive was repulsed](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_the_Piave_River "Second Battle of the Piave River") in June 1918. On 24 October, Diaz launched the [Battle of Vittorio Veneto](/wiki/Battle_of_Vittorio_Veneto "Battle of Vittorio Veneto") and initially met stubborn resistance,[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert1994482-143) but with Austria-Hungary collapsing, Hungarian divisions in Italy demanded they be sent home.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert1994484-144) When this was granted, many others followed and the Imperial army disintegrated, the Italians taking over 300,000 prisoners.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThompson2009364-145) On 3 November, the [Armistice of Villa Giusti](/wiki/Armistice_of_Villa_Giusti "Armistice of Villa Giusti") ended hostilities between Austria-Hungary and Italy which occupied [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste") and areas along the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea") awarded to it in 1915.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert1994491-146)

### Eastern Front

Main article: [Eastern Front (World War I)](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_I) "Eastern Front (World War I)")

#### Initial actions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Mikolaj_II_w_Twierdzy_Przemysl.jpg/250px-Mikolaj_II_w_Twierdzy_Przemysl.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mikolaj_II_w_Twierdzy_Przemysl.jpg)

Tsar [Nicholas II](/wiki/Nicholas_II_of_Russia "Nicholas II of Russia") and [Grand Duke Nikolaevich](/wiki/Grand_Duke_Nicholas_Nikolaevich_of_Russia_(1856%E2%80%931929) "Grand Duke Nicholas Nikolaevich of Russia (1856–1929)") following the Russian [capture of Przemyśl](/wiki/Siege_of_Przemy%C5%9Bl "Siege of Przemyśl"), the longest siege of the war

As previously agreed with French president [Raymond Poincaré](/wiki/Raymond_Poincar%C3%A9 "Raymond Poincaré"), Russian plans at the start of the war were to simultaneously advance into [Austrian Galicia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia_and_Lodomeria "Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria") and East Prussia as soon as possible. Although their [attack on Galicia](/wiki/Battle_of_Galicia "Battle of Galicia") was largely successful, and the invasions achieved their aim of forcing Germany to divert troops from the Western Front, the speed of mobilisation meant they did so without much of their heavy equipment and support functions. These weaknesses contributed to Russian defeats at [Tannenberg](/wiki/Battle_of_Tannenberg "Battle of Tannenberg") and the [Masurian Lakes](/wiki/First_Battle_of_the_Masurian_Lakes "First Battle of the Masurian Lakes") in August and September 1914, forcing them to withdraw from East Prussia with heavy losses.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts2005715-147)[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeyer2006152–154,_161,_163,_175,_182-148) By spring 1915, they had also retreated from Galicia, and the May 1915 [Gorlice–Tarnów offensive](/wiki/Gorlice%E2%80%93Tarn%C3%B3w_offensive "Gorlice–Tarnów offensive") allowed the Central Powers to invade [Russian-occupied Poland](/wiki/Congress_Poland "Congress Poland").[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmele2011-149)

Despite the successful June 1916 [Brusilov offensive](/wiki/Brusilov_offensive "Brusilov offensive") against the Austrians in eastern Galicia,[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchindler2003?-150) shortages of supplies, heavy losses and command failures prevented the Russians from fully exploiting their victory. However, it was one of the most significant offensives of the war, diverting German resources from [Verdun](/wiki/Battle_of_Verdun "Battle of Verdun"), relieving Austro-Hungarian pressure on the Italians, and convincing Romania to enter the war on the side of the Allies on 27 August. It also fatally weakened both the Austrian and Russian armies, whose offensive capabilities were badly affected by their losses and increased disillusion with the war that ultimately led to the Russian revolutions.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETucker2002119-151)

Meanwhile, unrest grew in Russia as Tsar [Nicholas II](/wiki/Nicholas_II "Nicholas II") remained at the front, with the home front controlled by [Empress Alexandra](/wiki/Alexandra_Feodorovna_(Alix_of_Hesse) "Alexandra Feodorovna (Alix of Hesse)"). Her increasingly incompetent rule and food shortages in urban areas led to widespread protests and the murder of her favourite, [Grigori Rasputin](/wiki/Grigori_Rasputin "Grigori Rasputin"), at the end of 1916.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodrich2011376-152)

Romania key locations 1916–1918 (using 2026 borders)

Despite secretly agreeing to support the Triple Alliance in 1883, Romania increasingly found itself at odds with the Central Powers over their support for Bulgaria in the Balkan Wars and the status of ethnic Romanian communities in [Hungarian](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary")-controlled [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"),[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJelavich1992441–442-153) which comprised an estimated 2.8 million of the region's 5.0 million population.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDumitru2012171-154) With the ruling elite split into pro-German and pro-Entente factions,[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDumitru2012170-155) Romania remained neutral for two years while allowing Germany and Austria to transport military supplies and advisors across Romanian territory.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert1994282-156)

In September 1914, Russia acknowledged Romanian rights to Austro-Hungarian territories including Transylvania and [Banat](/wiki/Banat "Banat"), whose acquisition had widespread popular support,[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDumitru2012171-154) and Russian success against Austria led Romania to join the Entente in the August 1916 [Treaty of Bucharest](/wiki/Treaty_of_Bucharest_(1916) "Treaty of Bucharest (1916)").[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert1994282-156) Under the strategic plan known as [Hypothesis Z](/wiki/Hypothesis_Z "Hypothesis Z"), the Romanian army planned an offensive into Transylvania, while defending Southern [Dobruja](/wiki/Dobruja "Dobruja") and [Giurgiu](/wiki/Giurgiu_County "Giurgiu County") against a possible Bulgarian counterattack.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETorrie19787–8-157) On 27 August 1916, they [attacked Transylvania](/wiki/Battle_of_Transylvania "Battle of Transylvania") and occupied substantial parts of the province before being driven back by the recently formed [German 9th Army](/wiki/9th_Army_(German_Empire) "9th Army (German Empire)"), led by former Chief of Staff [Erich von Falkenhayn](/wiki/Erich_von_Falkenhayn "Erich von Falkenhayn").[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarrett201396–98-158) A combined German-Bulgarian-Turkish offensive captured Dobruja and Giurgiu, although the bulk of the Romanian army managed to escape encirclement and retreated to [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest"), which [surrendered](/wiki/Battle_of_Bucharest "Battle of Bucharest") to the Central Powers on 6 December 1916.[[155]](#cite_note-159)

In the summer of 1917, a Central Powers offensive began in Romania under the command of August von Mackensen to knock Romania out of the war, resulting in the battles of [Oituz](/wiki/Third_Battle_of_Oituz "Third Battle of Oituz"), [Mărăști](/wiki/Battle_of_M%C4%83r%C4%83%C8%99ti "Battle of Mărăști") and [Mărășești](/wiki/Battle_of_M%C4%83r%C4%83%C8%99e%C8%99ti "Battle of Mărășești"), where up to 1,000,000 Central Powers troops were present. The battles lasted from 22 July to 3 September and eventually the Romanian army was victorious advancing 500 km2. August von Mackensen could not plan for another offensive as he had to transfer troops to the Italian Front.[[156]](#cite_note-160) Following the Russian revolution, Romania found itself alone on the Eastern Front and signed the [Treaty of Bucharest](/wiki/Treaty_of_Bucharest_(1918) "Treaty of Bucharest (1918)") with the Central Powers, which recognised Romanian sovereignty over [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia") in return for ceding control of passes in the Carpathian Mountains to Austria-Hungary and leasing its oil wells to Germany. Although approved by [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Romania "Parliament of Romania"), [King Ferdinand I](/wiki/Ferdinand_I_of_Romania "Ferdinand I of Romania") refused to sign it, hoping for an Allied victory in the west.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrampton199424–25-161) Romania re-entered the war on 10 November 1918, on the side of the Allies and the Treaty of Bucharest was formally annulled by the Armistice of 11 November 1918.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBéla1998429-162)[[e]](#cite_note-164)

### Central Powers peace overtures

On 12 December 1916, after ten brutal months of the [Battle of Verdun](/wiki/Battle_of_Verdun "Battle of Verdun") and a [successful offensive against Romania](/wiki/Romania_in_World_War_I#The_counteroffensive_of_the_Central_Powers "Romania in World War I"), Germany attempted to negotiate a peace with the Allies.[[160]](#cite_note-lanoszka-165) However, this attempt was rejected out of hand as a "duplicitous war ruse".[[160]](#cite_note-lanoszka-165)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/River_Crossing_NGM-v31-p338.jpg/250px-River_Crossing_NGM-v31-p338.jpg)](/wiki/File:River_Crossing_NGM-v31-p338.jpg)

"[They shall not pass](/wiki/They_shall_not_pass "They shall not pass")", a phrase typically associated with the defence of Verdun

US president [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") attempted to intervene as a peacemaker, asking for both sides to state their demands. [Lloyd George's](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George") War Cabinet considered the German offer to be a ploy to create divisions among the Allies. After initial outrage and much deliberation, they took Wilson's note as a separate effort, signalling that the US was on the verge of entering the war against Germany following the "submarine outrages". While the Allies debated a response to Wilson's offer, the Germans chose to rebuff it in favour of "a direct exchange of views". Learning of the German response, the Allied governments were free to make clear demands in their response of 14 January. They sought restoration of damages, the evacuation of occupied territories, reparations for France, Russia and Romania, and a recognition of the principle of nationalities.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeegan1998345-166) The Allies sought guarantees that would prevent or limit future wars.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKernek1970721–766-167) The negotiations failed and the Entente powers rejected the German offer on the grounds of honour, and noted Germany had not put forward any specific proposals.[[160]](#cite_note-lanoszka-165)

### Final years of the war

Main article: [Timeline of World War I (1917–1918)](/wiki/Timeline_of_World_War_I_(1917%E2%80%931918) "Timeline of World War I (1917–1918)")

#### Russian Revolution and withdrawal

Main articles: [February Revolution](/wiki/February_Revolution "February Revolution"), [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution"), and [Treaty of Brest-Litovsk](/wiki/Treaty_of_Brest-Litovsk "Treaty of Brest-Litovsk")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Map_Treaty_of_Brest-Litovsk-en.jpg/250px-Map_Treaty_of_Brest-Litovsk-en.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_Treaty_of_Brest-Litovsk-en.jpg)

Territory lost by Russia under the 1918 [Treaty of Brest-Litovsk](/wiki/Treaty_of_Brest-Litovsk "Treaty of Brest-Litovsk")

By the end of 1916, Russian casualties totalled nearly five million killed, wounded or captured, with major urban areas affected by food shortages and high prices. In March 1917, Tsar Nicholas ordered the military to forcibly suppress strikes in [Petrograd](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg") but the troops refused to fire on the crowds.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeckett2007523-168) Revolutionaries set up the [Petrograd Soviet](/wiki/Petrograd_Soviet "Petrograd Soviet") and fearing a left-wing takeover, the [State Duma](/wiki/State_Duma "State Duma") forced Nicholas to abdicate and established the [Russian Provisional Government](/wiki/Russian_Provisional_Government "Russian Provisional Government"), which confirmed Russia's willingness to continue the war. However, the Petrograd Soviet refused to disband, creating [competing power centres](/wiki/Dual_power "Dual power") and causing confusion and chaos, with frontline soldiers becoming increasingly demoralised.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWinter2014110–132-169)

Following the tsar's abdication, [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin")—with the help of the German government—was ushered from Switzerland into Russia on 16 April 1917. Discontent and the weaknesses of the Provisional Government led to a rise in the popularity of the Bolshevik Party, led by Lenin, which demanded an immediate end to the war. The Revolution of November was followed in December by an armistice and negotiations with Germany. At first, the Bolsheviks refused the German terms, but when German troops began marching across Ukraine unopposed, they acceded to the [Treaty of Brest-Litovsk](/wiki/Treaty_of_Brest-Litovsk "Treaty of Brest-Litovsk") on 3 March 1918. The treaty ceded vast territories, including Finland, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, and parts of Poland and Ukraine to the Central Powers.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWheeler-Bennett193836–41-170)

With the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") out of the war, Romania found itself alone on the Eastern Front and signed the [Treaty of Bucharest](/wiki/Treaty_of_Bucharest_(1918) "Treaty of Bucharest (1918)") with the Central Powers in May 1918. Under the terms of the treaty, Romania ceded territory to Austria-Hungary and Bulgaria and leased its oil reserves to Germany. However, the terms also included the Central Powers' recognition of the union of [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia") with Romania.[[166]](#cite_note-171)[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrampton199424–25-161)

#### United States enters the war

Main article: [American entry into World War I](/wiki/American_entry_into_World_War_I "American entry into World War I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/President_Woodrow_Wilson_asking_Congress_to_declare_war_on_Germany%2C_2_April_1917.jpg/250px-President_Woodrow_Wilson_asking_Congress_to_declare_war_on_Germany%2C_2_April_1917.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Woodrow_Wilson_asking_Congress_to_declare_war_on_Germany,_2_April_1917.jpg)

[President Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") asking [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") to declare war on Germany, 2 April 1917

The United States was a major supplier of war [materiel](/wiki/Materiel "Materiel") to the Allies but remained neutral in 1914, in large part due to domestic opposition.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStevenson2012315–316-172) The most significant factor in creating the support Wilson needed was the German submarine offensive, which not only cost American lives but paralysed trade as ships were reluctant to put to sea.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStevenson2012317-173)

On 6 April 1917, Congress [declared war on Germany](/wiki/United_States_declaration_of_war_on_Germany_(1917) "United States declaration of war on Germany (1917)") as an "Associated Power" of the Allies.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert1994318-174) The [US Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") sent a [battleship group](/wiki/United_States_Battleship_Division_Nine_(World_War_I) "United States Battleship Division Nine (World War I)") to [Scapa Flow](/wiki/Scapa_Flow "Scapa Flow") to join the Grand Fleet, and provided convoy escorts. In April 1917, the [US Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") had fewer than 300,000 men, including [National Guard](/wiki/Army_National_Guard "Army National Guard") units, compared to British and French armies of 4.1 and 8.3 million respectively. The [Selective Service Act of 1917](/wiki/Selective_Service_Act_of_1917 "Selective Service Act of 1917") drafted 2.8 million men, though training and equipping such numbers was a huge logistical challenge. By June 1918, over 667,000 members of the [American Expeditionary Forces](/wiki/American_Expeditionary_Forces "American Expeditionary Forces") (AEF) were transported to France, a figure which reached 2 million by the end of November.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrotelueschen200614–15-175)

Despite his conviction that Germany must be defeated, Wilson went to war to ensure the US played a leading role in shaping the peace, which meant preserving the AEF as a separate military force, rather than being absorbed into British or French units as his Allies wanted.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStevenson2012318-176) He was strongly supported by AEF commander General [John J. Pershing](/wiki/John_J._Pershing "John J. Pershing"), a proponent of pre-1914 "open warfare" who considered the French and British emphasis on artillery misguided and incompatible with American "offensive spirit".[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrotelueschen200644–46-177) Much to the frustration of his Allies, who had suffered heavy losses in 1917, he insisted on retaining control of American troops, and refused to commit them to the front line until able to operate as independent units. As a result, the first significant US involvement was the [Meuse–Argonne offensive](/wiki/Meuse%E2%80%93Argonne_offensive "Meuse–Argonne offensive") in late September 1918.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStevenson2012403-178)

#### Nivelle Offensive (April–May 1917)

Further information: [Nivelle offensive](/wiki/Nivelle_offensive "Nivelle offensive") and [1917 French Army mutinies](/wiki/1917_French_Army_mutinies "1917 French Army mutinies")

[![Files of soldiers with rifles slung follow close behind a tank, there is a dead body in the foreground](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Canadian_tank_and_soldiers_Vimy_1917.jpg/250px-Canadian_tank_and_soldiers_Vimy_1917.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canadian_tank_and_soldiers_Vimy_1917.jpg)

[Canadian Corps](/wiki/Canadian_Corps "Canadian Corps") troops at the [Battle of Vimy Ridge](/wiki/Battle_of_Vimy_Ridge "Battle of Vimy Ridge"), 1917

In December 1916, [Robert Nivelle](/wiki/Robert_Nivelle "Robert Nivelle") replaced Pétain as commander of French armies on the Western Front and began planning a [spring attack](/wiki/Nivelle_offensive "Nivelle offensive") in [Champagne](/wiki/Champagne_(province) "Champagne (province)"), part of a joint Franco-British operation.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClayton2003124-179) Poor security meant German intelligence was well informed on tactics and timetables, but despite this, when the attack began on 16 April the French made substantial gains, before being brought to a halt by the newly built and extremely strong defences of the Hindenburg Line. Nivelle persisted with frontal assaults and, by 25 April, the French had suffered nearly 135,000 casualties, including 30,000 dead, most incurred in the first two days.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClayton2003129-180)

Concurrent British attacks at [Arras](/wiki/Battle_of_Arras_(1917) "Battle of Arras (1917)") were more successful, though ultimately of little strategic value.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrachan2003244-181) Operating as a separate unit for the first time, the [Canadian Corps](/wiki/Canadian_Corps "Canadian Corps")' capture of [Vimy Ridge](/wiki/Battle_of_Vimy_Ridge "Battle of Vimy Ridge") is viewed by many Canadians as a defining moment in creating a sense of national identity.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInglis19952-182)[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHumphries200766-183) Though Nivelle continued the offensive, on 3 May the [21st Division](/wiki/21st_Infantry_Division_(France) "21st Infantry Division (France)"), which had been involved in some of the heaviest fighting at Verdun, refused orders to go into battle, initiating the [French Army mutinies](/wiki/1917_French_Army_mutinies "1917 French Army mutinies"); within days, "collective indiscipline" had spread to 54 divisions, while over 20,000 deserted.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHorne1964323-184)

#### Sinai and Palestine campaign (1917–1918)

Main article: [Sinai and Palestine campaign](/wiki/Sinai_and_Palestine_campaign "Sinai and Palestine campaign")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Capture_of_Jerusalem_1917d.jpg/250px-Capture_of_Jerusalem_1917d.jpg)](/wiki/File:Capture_of_Jerusalem_1917d.jpg)

British artillery battery on [Mount Scopus](/wiki/Mount_Scopus "Mount Scopus") in the [Battle of Jerusalem](/wiki/Battle_of_Jerusalem "Battle of Jerusalem"), 1917.

In March and April 1917, at the [First](/wiki/First_Battle_of_Gaza "First Battle of Gaza") and [Second Battles of Gaza](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Gaza "Second Battle of Gaza"), German and Ottoman forces stopped the advance of the Egyptian Expeditionary Force, which had begun in August 1916 at the Battle of Romani.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErickson2001163-185)[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoore192067-186) At the end of October 1917, the [Sinai and Palestine campaign](/wiki/Sinai_and_Palestine_campaign "Sinai and Palestine campaign") resumed, when General [Edmund Allenby](/wiki/Edmund_Allenby,_1st_Viscount_Allenby "Edmund Allenby, 1st Viscount Allenby")'s [XXth Corps](/wiki/XX_Corps_(United_Kingdom) "XX Corps (United Kingdom)"), [XXI Corps](/wiki/XXI_Corps_(United_Kingdom) "XXI Corps (United Kingdom)") and [Desert Mounted Corps](/wiki/Desert_Mounted_Corps "Desert Mounted Corps") won the [Battle of Beersheba](/wiki/Battle_of_Beersheba_(1917) "Battle of Beersheba (1917)").[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFalls193059-187) Two Ottoman armies were defeated a few weeks later at the [Battle of Mughar Ridge](/wiki/Battle_of_Mughar_Ridge "Battle of Mughar Ridge") and, early in December, [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") had been captured following another Ottoman defeat at the [Battle of Jerusalem](/wiki/Battle_of_Jerusalem "Battle of Jerusalem").[[183]](#cite_note-188)[[184]](#cite_note-189)[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBruce2002162-190)

About this time, [Friedrich Freiherr Kress von Kressenstein](/wiki/Friedrich_Freiherr_Kress_von_Kressenstein "Friedrich Freiherr Kress von Kressenstein") was relieved of his duties as the Eighth Army's commander, replaced by [Djevad Pasha](/wiki/Cevat_%C3%87obanl%C4%B1 "Cevat Çobanlı"), and a few months later the commander of the [Ottoman Army](/wiki/Military_of_the_Ottoman_Empire "Military of the Ottoman Empire") in Palestine, [Erich von Falkenhayn](/wiki/Erich_von_Falkenhayn "Erich von Falkenhayn"), was replaced by [Otto Liman von Sanders](/wiki/Otto_Liman_von_Sanders "Otto Liman von Sanders").[[186]](#cite_note-191)[[187]](#cite_note-192)

In early 1918, the front line was [extended](/wiki/Capture_of_Jericho "Capture of Jericho") and the [Jordan Valley](/wiki/British_occupation_of_the_Jordan_Valley "British occupation of the Jordan Valley") was occupied, following the [First Transjordan](/wiki/First_Transjordan_attack_on_Amman "First Transjordan attack on Amman") and the [Second Transjordan](/wiki/Second_Transjordan_attack_on_Shunet_Nimrin_and_Es_Salt "Second Transjordan attack on Shunet Nimrin and Es Salt") attacks by British Empire forces in March and April 1918.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErickson2001195-193)

#### German offensive and Allied counter-offensive (March–November 1918)

Main articles: [German spring offensive](/wiki/German_spring_offensive "German spring offensive") and [Hundred Days Offensive](/wiki/Hundred_Days_Offensive "Hundred Days Offensive")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Riflemen-1918-Western-Front.png/250px-Riflemen-1918-Western-Front.png)](/wiki/File:Riflemen-1918-Western-Front.png)

Between April and November 1918, the Allies increased their front-line rifle strength while German strength fell by half.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAyers1919104-194)

In December 1917, the Central Powers signed an armistice with Russia, thus freeing large numbers of German troops for use in the West. With German reinforcements and new American troops pouring in, the outcome was to be decided on the Western Front. The Central Powers knew that they could not win a protracted war, but they held high hopes for success in a final quick offensive.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeyman1997146–147-195) Ludendorff drew up plans ([Operation Michael](/wiki/Operation_Michael "Operation Michael")) for the 1918 offensive on the Western Front. The operation commenced on 21 March 1918, with an attack on British forces near [Saint-Quentin](/wiki/Saint-Quentin,_Aisne "Saint-Quentin, Aisne"). German forces achieved an unprecedented advance of 60 kilometres (37 mi).[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWestwell2004-196) The initial offensive was a success; after heavy fighting, however, the offensive was halted. Lacking tanks or [motorised artillery](/wiki/Self-propelled_artillery "Self-propelled artillery"), the Germans were unable to consolidate their gains. The problems of re-supply were also exacerbated by increasing distances that now stretched over terrain that was shell-torn and often impassable to traffic.[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGray199186-197)
Germany launched [Operation Georgette](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Lys_(1918) "Battle of the Lys (1918)") against the northern [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel") ports. The Allies halted the drive after limited territorial gains by Germany. The German Army to the south then conducted [Operations Blücher and Yorck](/wiki/Third_Battle_of_the_Aisne "Third Battle of the Aisne"), pushing broadly towards Paris. Germany launched Operation Marne ([Second Battle of the Marne](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_the_Marne "Second Battle of the Marne")) on 15 July, in an attempt to encircle [Reims](/wiki/Reims "Reims"). The resulting counter-attack, which started the [Hundred Days Offensive](/wiki/Hundred_Days_Offensive "Hundred Days Offensive") on 8 August,[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERickard2007-198) led to a marked collapse in German morale.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchreiber200450-199)[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERickard2001-200)[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown1999190-201)

#### Allied advance to the Hindenburg Line

See also: [Meuse-Argonne offensive](/wiki/Meuse-Argonne_offensive "Meuse-Argonne offensive")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/US23rdInfantry37mmGunInActionFrance1918-ARC531005.gif/250px-US23rdInfantry37mmGunInActionFrance1918-ARC531005.gif)](/wiki/File:US23rdInfantry37mmGunInActionFrance1918-ARC531005.gif)

American soldiers firing on German entrenched positions during the Meuse-Argonne offensive, 1918

By September, the Germans had fallen back to the Hindenburg Line. The Allies had [advanced to the Hindenburg Line](/wiki/Hundred_Days_Offensive#Advance_to_the_Hindenburg_Line "Hundred Days Offensive") in the north and centre. German forces launched numerous counterattacks, but positions and outposts of the Line continued falling, with the BEF alone taking 30,441 prisoners in the last week of September. On 24 September, the Supreme Army Command informed the leaders in Berlin that armistice talks were inevitable.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrayArgyle1990-202)

The [final assault](/wiki/Grand_Offensive "Grand Offensive") on the Hindenburg Line began with the [Meuse-Argonne offensive](/wiki/Meuse-Argonne_offensive "Meuse-Argonne offensive"), launched by American and French troops on 26 September. Two days later the Belgians, French and British [attacked around Ypres](/wiki/Fifth_Battle_of_Ypres "Fifth Battle of Ypres"), and the day after the British at St Quentin in the centre of the line. The following week, cooperating American and French units broke through in [Champagne](/wiki/Champagne_(province) "Champagne (province)") at the [Battle of Blanc Mont Ridge](/wiki/Battle_of_Blanc_Mont_Ridge "Battle of Blanc Mont Ridge") ( 3–27 October), forcing the Germans off the commanding heights, and closing towards the Belgian frontier.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcLellan49-203) On 8 October, the Hindenburg Line was pierced by British and Dominion troops of the First and Third British Armies at the [Second Battle of Cambrai](/wiki/Battle_of_Cambrai_(1918) "Battle of Cambrai (1918)").[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChristie1997?-204)

#### Breakthrough of Macedonian front (September 1918)

Main articles: [Vardar offensive](/wiki/Vardar_offensive "Vardar offensive") and [Battle of Dobro Pole](/wiki/Battle_of_Dobro_Pole "Battle of Dobro Pole")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Bulgarian_major_Ivanov_with_white_flag_surrendering_to_Serbian_7th_Danube_ragiment.jpg/250px-Bulgarian_major_Ivanov_with_white_flag_surrendering_to_Serbian_7th_Danube_ragiment.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bulgarian_major_Ivanov_with_white_flag_surrendering_to_Serbian_7th_Danube_ragiment.jpg)

Bulgarian major Ivanov with white flag surrendering to Serbian 7th Danube regiment near [Kumanovo](/wiki/Kumanovo "Kumanovo")

Allied forces started the [Vardar offensive](/wiki/Vardar_offensive "Vardar offensive") on 15 September at two key points: [Dobro Pole](/wiki/Dobro_Pole "Dobro Pole") and near [Dojran Lake](/wiki/Dojran_Lake "Dojran Lake"). In the [Battle of Dobro Pole](/wiki/Battle_of_Dobro_Pole "Battle of Dobro Pole"), the Serbian and French armies had success after a three-day-long battle with relatively small casualties, and subsequently made a breakthrough in the front, something which was rarely seen in World War I. After the front was broken, Allied forces started to liberate Serbia and reached [Skopje](/wiki/Skopje "Skopje") at 29 September, after which [Bulgaria](/wiki/Tsardom_of_Bulgaria_(1908%E2%80%931946) "Tsardom of Bulgaria (1908–1946)") signed an armistice with the Allies on 30 September.[[200]](#cite_note-militaryhistorynow-205)[[201]](#cite_note-206)

### Armistices and capitulations

Main articles: [Armistice of Salonica](/wiki/Armistice_of_Salonica "Armistice of Salonica"), [Armistice of Villa Giusti](/wiki/Armistice_of_Villa_Giusti "Armistice of Villa Giusti"), and [Armistice of Mudros](/wiki/Armistice_of_Mudros "Armistice of Mudros")

Further information: [Armistice of Belgrade](/wiki/Armistice_of_Belgrade "Armistice of Belgrade")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Trento_3_novembre_1918.jpg/250px-Trento_3_novembre_1918.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trento_3_novembre_1918.jpg)

Italian troops reach [Trento](/wiki/Trento "Trento") during the [Battle of Vittorio Veneto](/wiki/Battle_of_Vittorio_Veneto "Battle of Vittorio Veneto"), 1918

The collapse of the Central Powers came swiftly. Bulgaria was the first to sign an armistice, the [Armistice of Salonica](/wiki/Armistice_of_Salonica "Armistice of Salonica") on 29 September 1918.[[202]](#cite_note-indiana.edu-1918-207) Wilhelm II, in a telegram to Tsar [Ferdinand I of Bulgaria](/wiki/Ferdinand_I_of_Bulgaria "Ferdinand I of Bulgaria") described the situation thus: "Disgraceful! 62,000 Serbs decided the war!".[[200]](#cite_note-militaryhistorynow-205)[[203]](#cite_note-208) On the same day, the [German Supreme Army Command](/wiki/Oberste_Heeresleitung "Oberste Heeresleitung") informed Wilhelm II and the [Imperial Chancellor](/wiki/Imperial_Chancellor_(Germany) "Imperial Chancellor (Germany)") Count [Georg von Hertling](/wiki/Georg_von_Hertling "Georg von Hertling"), that the military situation facing Germany was hopeless.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAxelrod2018260-209)

On 24 October, the Italians began a push that rapidly recovered territory lost after the Battle of Caporetto. This culminated in the Battle of Vittorio Veneto, marking the end of the Austro-Hungarian Army as an effective fighting force. The offensive also triggered the disintegration of the Austro-Hungarian Empire. During the last week of October, declarations of independence were made in Budapest, Prague, and Zagreb. On 29 October, the imperial authorities asked Italy for an armistice, but the Italians continued advancing, reaching Trento, Udine, and Trieste. On 3 November, Austria-Hungary sent a [flag of truce](/wiki/White_flag "White flag") and accepted the [Armistice of Villa Giusti](/wiki/Armistice_of_Villa_Giusti "Armistice of Villa Giusti"), arranged with the Allied Authorities in Paris. Austria and Hungary signed separate armistices following the overthrow of the [Habsburg monarchy](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"). In the following days, the Italian Army occupied [Innsbruck](/wiki/Innsbruck "Innsbruck") and all [Tyrol](/wiki/Tyrol "Tyrol"), with over 20,000 soldiers.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEdi_Michele2014436–437-210) On 30 October, the Ottoman Empire capitulated, and signed the Armistice of Mudros.[[202]](#cite_note-indiana.edu-1918-207)

#### German government surrenders

Main article: [Armistice of 11 November 1918](/wiki/Armistice_of_11_November_1918 "Armistice of 11 November 1918")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Armisticetrain.jpg/250px-Armisticetrain.jpg)](/wiki/File:Armisticetrain.jpg)

[Ferdinand Foch](/wiki/Ferdinand_Foch "Ferdinand Foch") (*second from right*) pictured outside the [carriage](/wiki/Compi%C3%A8gne_Wagon "Compiègne Wagon") in [Compiègne](/wiki/Compi%C3%A8gne "Compiègne") after agreeing to the [armistice](/wiki/Armistice_of_11_November_1918 "Armistice of 11 November 1918") that ended the war there.[[206]](#cite_note-211)

With the military faltering and with widespread loss of confidence in the kaiser, Germany moved towards surrender. Prince Maximilian of Baden took charge on 3 October as Chancellor of Germany. Negotiations with President Wilson began immediately, in the hope that he would offer better terms than the British and French. Wilson demanded a constitutional monarchy and parliamentary control over the German military.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStevenson2004385-212)

The [German Revolution of 1918–1919](/wiki/German_Revolution_of_1918%E2%80%931919 "German Revolution of 1918–1919") began at the end of October 1918. Units of the German Navy refused to set sail for a large-scale operation in a war they believed to be as good as lost. The [sailors' revolt](/wiki/Kiel_mutiny "Kiel mutiny"), which then ensued in the naval ports of [Wilhelmshaven](/wiki/Wilhelmshaven "Wilhelmshaven") and [Kiel](/wiki/Kiel "Kiel"), spread across the whole country within days and led to the [proclamation of a republic](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_republic_in_Germany "Proclamation of the republic in Germany") on 9 November 1918, shortly thereafter to the [abdication of Wilhelm II](/wiki/Abdication_of_Wilhelm_II "Abdication of Wilhelm II"), and German surrender.[[208]](#cite_note-213)[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDähnhardt197891-214)[[210]](#cite_note-215)[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStevenson2004383-216)[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStevenson2004Chapter_17-217)

## Aftermath

Main article: [Aftermath of World War I](/wiki/Aftermath_of_World_War_I "Aftermath of World War I")

In the aftermath of the war, the German, Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman, and Russian empires disappeared.[[f]](#cite_note-218) Numerous nations regained their former independence, and new ones were created. Four dynasties fell as a result of the war: the [Romanovs](/wiki/House_of_Romanov "House of Romanov"), the [Hohenzollerns](/wiki/House_of_Hohenzollern "House of Hohenzollern"), the [Habsburgs](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg"), and the [Ottomans](/wiki/Ottoman_dynasty "Ottoman dynasty"). Belgium and Serbia were badly damaged, as was France, with 1.4 million soldiers dead,[[213]](#cite_note-219) not counting other casualties. Germany and Russia were similarly affected.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts2005[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid2YqjfHLyyj8CpgPA273_273]-220)

### Formal end of the war

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/William_Orpen_-_The_Signing_of_Peace_in_the_Hall_of_Mirrors.jpg/250px-William_Orpen_-_The_Signing_of_Peace_in_the_Hall_of_Mirrors.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Orpen_-_The_Signing_of_Peace_in_the_Hall_of_Mirrors.jpg)

The signing of the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") in the [Hall of Mirrors](/wiki/Hall_of_Mirrors "Hall of Mirrors"), Versailles, 28 June 1919, by Sir [William Orpen](/wiki/William_Orpen "William Orpen")

A formal state of war between the two sides persisted for another seven months, until the signing of the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") with Germany on 28 June 1919. The US Senate did not ratify the treaty despite public support for it,[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHastedt2009483-221)[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurrinJohnsonMcPhersonGerstle2010622-222)and did not formally end its involvement in the war until the [Knox–Porter Resolution](/wiki/Knox%E2%80%93Porter_Resolution "Knox–Porter Resolution") was signed on 2 July 1921 by President [Warren G. Harding](/wiki/Warren_G._Harding "Warren G. Harding").[[217]](#cite_note-223) For the British Empire, the state of war ceased under the provisions of the *[Termination of the Present War (Definition) Act 1918](/wiki/Termination_of_the_Present_War_(Definition)_Act_1918 "Termination of the Present War (Definition) Act 1918")* concerning:

:   * Germany on 10 January 1920.[[218]](#cite_note-224)
    * Austria on 16 July 1920.[[219]](#cite_note-225)
    * Bulgaria on 9 August 1920.[[220]](#cite_note-226)
    * Hungary on 26 July 1921.[[221]](#cite_note-227)
    * Turkey on 6 August 1924.[[222]](#cite_note-228)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Venizelos_signing_the_Treaty_of_Sevres.jpeg/250px-Venizelos_signing_the_Treaty_of_Sevres.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Venizelos_signing_the_Treaty_of_Sevres.jpeg)

Greek prime minister [Eleftherios Venizelos](/wiki/Eleftherios_Venizelos "Eleftherios Venizelos") signing the [Treaty of Sèvres](/wiki/Treaty_of_S%C3%A8vres "Treaty of Sèvres")

Some [war memorials](/wiki/War_memorial "War memorial") date the end of the war as being when the Versailles Treaty was signed in 1919, which was when many of the troops serving abroad finally returned home; by contrast, most commemorations of the war's end concentrate on the armistice of 11 November 1918.[[223]](#cite_note-229)

### Peace treaties and national boundaries

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Map_Europe_1923-en.svg/330px-Map_Europe_1923-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_Europe_1923-en.svg)

Map of [territorial changes in Europe](/wiki/List_of_national_border_changes_(1914%E2%80%93present) "List of national border changes (1914–present)") after World War I (as of 1923)

The [Paris Peace Conference](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Conference_(1919%E2%80%931920) "Paris Peace Conference (1919–1920)") imposed a series of peace treaties on the Central Powers officially ending the war. The 1919 [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") dealt with Germany and, building on [Wilson's 14th point](/wiki/Fourteen_Points "Fourteen Points"), established the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") on 28 June 1919.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagliveras19998–12-230)[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorthedge198635–36-231)

The Central Powers had to acknowledge responsibility for "all the loss and damage to which the Allied and Associated Governments and their nationals have been subjected as a consequence of the war imposed upon them by" their aggression. In the Treaty of Versailles, this statement was [Article 231](/wiki/Article_231_of_the_Treaty_of_Versailles "Article 231 of the Treaty of Versailles"). This article became known as the "War Guilt Clause", as the majority of Germans felt humiliated and resentful.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorrow2005290-232) The Germans felt they had been unjustly dealt with by what they called the "[diktat](/wiki/Diktat "Diktat") of Versailles". German historian Hagen Schulze said the Treaty placed Germany "under legal sanctions, deprived of military power, economically ruined, and politically humiliated."[[227]](#cite_note-233) Belgian historian Laurence Van Ypersele emphasises the central role played by memory of the war and the Versailles Treaty in German politics in the 1920s and 1930s:

> Active denial of war guilt in Germany and German resentment at both reparations and continued Allied occupation of the Rhineland made widespread revision of the meaning and memory of the war problematic. The legend of the "[stab in the back](/wiki/Stab-in-the-back_myth "Stab-in-the-back myth")" and the wish to revise the "Versailles diktat", and the belief in an international threat aimed at the elimination of the German nation persisted at the heart of German politics. Even a man of peace such as [[Gustav] Stresemann](/wiki/Gustav_Stresemann "Gustav Stresemann") publicly rejected German guilt. As for the Nazis, they waved the banners of domestic treason and international conspiracy in an attempt to galvanise the German nation into a spirit of revenge. Like a Fascist Italy, Nazi Germany sought to redirect the memory of the war to the benefit of its policies.[[228]](#cite_note-234)

Meanwhile, new nations liberated from German rule viewed the treaty as a recognition of wrongs committed against small nations by much larger aggressive neighbours.[[229]](#cite_note-235)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Dissolution_of_Austria-Hungary.png/330px-Dissolution_of_Austria-Hungary.png)](/wiki/File:Dissolution_of_Austria-Hungary.png)

[Dissolution of Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Dissolution_of_Austria-Hungary "Dissolution of Austria-Hungary") after war

Austria-Hungary was partitioned into several successor states, largely but not entirely along ethnic lines. Apart from Austria and Hungary, Czechoslovakia, Italy, Poland, Romania and Yugoslavia received territories from the Dual Monarchy (the formerly separate and autonomous [Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia-Slavonia "Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia") was incorporated into Yugoslavia). The details were contained in the treaties of [Saint-Germain-en-Laye](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint-Germain-en-Laye_(1919) "Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye (1919)") and [Trianon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Trianon "Treaty of Trianon"). As a result, Hungary lost 64% of its total population, decreasing from 20.9 million to 7.6 million, and losing 31% (3.3 out of 10.7 million) of its ethnic [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarian_people "Hungarian people").[[230]](#cite_note-236) According to the 1910 census, speakers of the Hungarian language included approximately 54% of the entire population of the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary"). Within the country, numerous ethnic minorities were present: 16.1% [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians"), 10.5% [Slovaks](/wiki/Slovaks "Slovaks"), 10.4% [Germans](/wiki/Germans "Germans"), 2.5% [Ruthenians](/wiki/Ruthenians "Ruthenians"), 2.5% [Serbs](/wiki/Serbs "Serbs"), and 8% others.[[231]](#cite_note-Frucht,_p._356-237) Between 1920 and 1924, 354,000 Hungarians fled former Hungarian territories attached to Romania, Czechoslovakia, and Yugoslavia.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKocsisHodosi199819-238)

The Russian Empire lost much of its western frontier as the newly independent nations of [Estonia](/wiki/History_of_Estonia#Road_to_the_republic "History of Estonia"), [Finland](/wiki/History_of_Finland#Independence_and_Civil_War "History of Finland"), [Latvia](/wiki/History_of_Latvia#Independence "History of Latvia"), [Lithuania](/wiki/History_of_Lithuania#Independent_Lithuania_(1918–40) "History of Lithuania"), and [Poland](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic") were carved from it. Romania took control of Bessarabia in April 1918.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark1927-239)

### National identities

Further information: [Sykes–Picot Agreement](/wiki/Sykes%E2%80%93Picot_Agreement "Sykes–Picot Agreement")

After 123 years, Poland re-emerged as an independent country. The Kingdom of Serbia and its dynasty, as a "minor Entente nation" and the country with the most casualties per capita,[[234]](#cite_note-240)[[235]](#cite_note-241)[[236]](#cite_note-242) became the backbone of a new multinational state, the [Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia"), later renamed Yugoslavia. Czechoslovakia, combining the [Kingdom of Bohemia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bohemia "Kingdom of Bohemia") with parts of the Kingdom of Hungary, became a new nation. Romania would [unite](/wiki/Great_Union "Great Union") all Romanian-speaking people under a single state, leading to [Greater Romania](/wiki/Greater_Romania "Greater Romania").[[237]](#cite_note-243)

In Australia and New Zealand, the Battle of Gallipoli became known as those nations' "Baptism of Fire". It was the first major war in which the newly established countries fought, and it was one of the first times that Australian troops fought as Australians, not just subjects of the [British Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown"), and independent national identities for these nations took hold. [Anzac Day](/wiki/Anzac_Day "Anzac Day"), named after the Australian and New Zealand Army Corps (ANZAC), commemorates this defining moment.[[238]](#cite_note-244)[[239]](#cite_note-awmtradition-245)

In the aftermath of World War I, Greece [fought](/wiki/Greco-Turkish_War_(1919%E2%80%931922) "Greco-Turkish War (1919–1922)") against Turkish nationalists led by [Mustafa Kemal](/wiki/Mustafa_Kemal_Atat%C3%BCrk "Mustafa Kemal Atatürk"), a war that eventually resulted in a [massive population exchange between the two countries](/wiki/Population_exchange_between_Greece_and_Turkey "Population exchange between Greece and Turkey") under the Treaty of Lausanne.[[240]](#cite_note-246) According to various sources,[[241]](#cite_note-247) several hundred thousand Greeks died during this period, which was tied in with the Greek genocide.[[242]](#cite_note-248)

## Casualties

Main article: [World War I casualties](/wiki/World_War_I_casualties "World War I casualties")

Further information: [Spanish flu](/wiki/Spanish_flu "Spanish flu")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Transporting_Ottoman_injured_at_Sirkedji.jpg/250px-Transporting_Ottoman_injured_at_Sirkedji.jpg)](/wiki/File:Transporting_Ottoman_injured_at_Sirkedji.jpg)

Men transporting a wounded Ottoman soldier at [Sirkeci](/wiki/Sirkeci "Sirkeci")

An estimated number of military and [civilian casualties](/wiki/Civilian_casualties "Civilian casualties") in World War I was about 15 to 22 million deaths[[243]](#cite_note-Britannica-249) and about 23 million wounded military personnel, ranking it among the deadliest conflicts in human history. The total number of deaths includes between 9 and 11 million [military personnel](/wiki/Military_personnel "Military personnel"), with an estimated civilian death toll of about 6 to 13 million.[[243]](#cite_note-Britannica-249)[[244]](#cite_note-250)

Of the 60 million European military personnel who were mobilised from 1914 to 1918, an estimated 8 million were killed, 7 million were permanently disabled, and 15 million were seriously injured. Germany lost 15.1% of its active male population, Austria-Hungary lost 17.1%, and France lost 10.5%.[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitchen200022-251) France mobilised 7.8 million men, of which 1.4 million died and 3.2 million were injured.[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESévillia2019395-252) Approximately 15,000 deployed men sustained gruesome facial injuries, causing social stigma and marginalisation; they were called the *[gueules cassées](/wiki/Gueules_cass%C3%A9es "Gueules cassées")* (broken faces). In Germany, civilian deaths were 474,000 higher than in peacetime, due in large part to food shortages and malnutrition that had weakened disease resistance. These excess deaths are estimated as 271,000 in 1918, plus another 71,000 in the first half of 1919 when the blockade was still in effect.[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward1993166-253) Starvation caused by famine killed approximately 100,000 people in Lebanon.[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaadi2009-254)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Emergency_hospital_during_Influenza_epidemic%2C_Camp_Funston%2C_Kansas_-_NCP_1603.jpg/250px-Emergency_hospital_during_Influenza_epidemic%2C_Camp_Funston%2C_Kansas_-_NCP_1603.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emergency_hospital_during_Influenza_epidemic,_Camp_Funston,_Kansas_-_NCP_1603.jpg)

Emergency military hospital during the [Spanish flu](/wiki/Spanish_flu "Spanish flu") pandemic in [Camp Funston](/wiki/Camp_Funston "Camp Funston"), [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas"), 1918

Diseases flourished in the chaotic wartime conditions. In 1914 alone, louse-borne [epidemic typhus](/wiki/Epidemic_typhus "Epidemic typhus") killed 200,000 in Serbia.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETschanz-255) Starting in early 1918, a major influenza epidemic known as [Spanish flu](/wiki/Spanish_flu "Spanish flu") spread across the world, accelerated by the movement of large numbers of soldiers, often crammed together in camps and transport ships with poor sanitation. The Spanish flu killed at least 17 to 25 million people,[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpreeuwenberg20182561–2567-256)[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnoblerMackMahmoudLemon2005-257) including an estimated 2.64 million Europeans and as many as 675,000 Americans.[[252]](#cite_note-Ansart_et_al._2009-258) Between 1915 and 1926, an epidemic of [encephalitis lethargica](/wiki/Encephalitis_lethargica "Encephalitis lethargica") affected nearly 5 million people worldwide.[[253]](#cite_note-259)[[254]](#cite_note-Reid_2001-260)

Eight million [equines](/wiki/Equus_(genus) "Equus (genus)"), mostly horses, donkeys and mules died, three-quarters of them from the extreme conditions they worked in.[[255]](#cite_note-261)

### War crimes

Main article: [War crimes in World War I](/wiki/War_crimes_in_World_War_I "War crimes in World War I")

#### Chemical weapons in warfare

Main article: [Chemical weapons in World War I](/wiki/Chemical_weapons_in_World_War_I "Chemical weapons in World War I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/French_soldiers_making_a_gas_and_flame_attack_on_German_trenches_in_Flanders._Belgium.%2C_ca._1900_-_1982_-_NARA_-_530722.tif/lossy-page1-250px-French_soldiers_making_a_gas_and_flame_attack_on_German_trenches_in_Flanders._Belgium.%2C_ca._1900_-_1982_-_NARA_-_530722.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:French_soldiers_making_a_gas_and_flame_attack_on_German_trenches_in_Flanders._Belgium.,_ca._1900_-_1982_-_NARA_-_530722.tif)

French soldiers making a gas and flame attack on German trenches in Flanders

The German army was the first to successfully deploy chemical weapons during the Second Battle of Ypres (April–May 1915), after German scientists under the direction of [Fritz Haber](/wiki/Fritz_Haber "Fritz Haber") at the [Kaiser Wilhelm Institute](/wiki/Kaiser_Wilhelm_Society "Kaiser Wilhelm Society") developed a method to weaponise [chlorine](/wiki/Chlorine "Chlorine").[[g]](#cite_note-263)[[257]](#cite_note-AJPH-264) The use of chemical weapons had been sanctioned by the German High Command to force Allied soldiers out of their entrenched positions, complementing rather than supplanting more lethal conventional weapons.[[257]](#cite_note-AJPH-264) Chemical weapons were deployed by all major belligerents throughout the war, inflicting approximately 1.3 million casualties, of which about 90,000 were fatal.[[257]](#cite_note-AJPH-264) The use of chemical weapons in warfare was a direct violation of the [1899 Hague Declaration Concerning Asphyxiating Gases](/wiki/Hague_Conventions_of_1899_and_1907#Hague_Convention_of_1899 "Hague Conventions of 1899 and 1907") and the [1907 Hague Convention on Land Warfare](/wiki/Hague_Conventions_of_1899_and_1907 "Hague Conventions of 1899 and 1907"), which prohibited their use.[[258]](#cite_note-265)[[259]](#cite_note-266)

#### Genocides by the Ottoman Empire

Main articles: [Armenian genocide](/wiki/Armenian_genocide "Armenian genocide"), [Assyrian genocide](/wiki/Assyrian_genocide "Assyrian genocide"), and [Greek genocide](/wiki/Greek_genocide "Greek genocide")

See also: [Late Ottoman genocides](/wiki/Late_Ottoman_genocides "Late Ottoman genocides") and [Armenian genocide denial](/wiki/Armenian_genocide_denial "Armenian genocide denial")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Ambassador_Morgenthau%27s_Story_p314.jpg/250px-Ambassador_Morgenthau%27s_Story_p314.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ambassador_Morgenthau%27s_Story_p314.jpg)

Armenians killed during the Armenian genocide. Image taken from *Ambassador Morgenthau's Story*, written by [Henry Morgenthau Sr.](/wiki/Henry_Morgenthau_Sr. "Henry Morgenthau Sr.") and published in 1918.[[260]](#cite_note-267)

The [ethnic cleansing](/wiki/Ethnic_cleansing "Ethnic cleansing") of the Ottoman Empire's Armenian population, including mass deportations and executions, during the final years of the Ottoman Empire is considered [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide").[[261]](#cite_note-IAGSletter-268) The Ottomans carried out organised and systematic massacres of the Armenian population at the beginning of the war and manipulated acts of Armenian resistance by portraying them as rebellions to justify further extermination.[[262]](#cite_note-leverkun-269) In early 1915, several Armenians volunteered to join the Russian forces and the Ottoman government used this as a pretext to issue the [Tehcir Law](/wiki/Temporary_Law_of_Deportation "Temporary Law of Deportation") (Law on Deportation), which authorised the deportation of Armenians from the Empire's eastern provinces to Syria between 1915 and 1918. The Armenians were intentionally [marched to death](/wiki/Death_march "Death march") and a number were attacked by Ottoman brigands.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerguson2006177-270) While the exact number of deaths is unknown, the [International Association of Genocide Scholars](/wiki/International_Association_of_Genocide_Scholars "International Association of Genocide Scholars") estimates around 1.5 million.[[261]](#cite_note-IAGSletter-268)[[264]](#cite_note-271) The government of Turkey continues to [deny the genocide](/wiki/Armenian_genocide_denial "Armenian genocide denial") to the present day, arguing that those who died were victims of inter-ethnic fighting, famine, or disease during World War I; these claims are rejected by most historians.[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFromkin1989212–215-272)

Other ethnic groups were similarly attacked by the Ottoman Empire during this period, including Assyrians and [Greeks](/wiki/Greek_genocide "Greek genocide"), and some scholars consider those events to be part of the same policy of extermination.[[266]](#cite_note-273)[[267]](#cite_note-274)[[268]](#cite_note-275) At least 250,000 Assyrian Christians, about half of the population, and 350,000–750,000 [Anatolian](/wiki/Greeks_in_Turkey "Greeks in Turkey") and [Pontic Greeks](/wiki/Pontic_Greeks "Pontic Greeks") were killed between 1915 and 1922.[[269]](#cite_note-276)

### Prisoners of war

Main article: [Prisoners of war in World War I](/wiki/Prisoners_of_war_in_World_War_I "Prisoners of war in World War I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/1stGazaBritishPrisoners00118v.jpg/250px-1stGazaBritishPrisoners00118v.jpg)](/wiki/File:1stGazaBritishPrisoners00118v.jpg)

British prisoners guarded by Ottoman forces after the [First Battle of Gaza](/wiki/First_Battle_of_Gaza "First Battle of Gaza") in 1917

About 8 million soldiers surrendered and were held in [POW camps](/wiki/Prisoner-of-war_camp "Prisoner-of-war camp") during the war. All nations pledged to follow the [Hague Conventions](/wiki/Hague_Conventions_of_1899_and_1907 "Hague Conventions of 1899 and 1907") on fair treatment of [prisoners of war](/wiki/Prisoner_of_war "Prisoner of war"), and the survival rate for POWs was generally much higher than that of combatants at the front.[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPhillimoreBellot19194–64-277)

Around 25–31% of Russian losses (as a proportion of those captured, wounded, or killed) were to prisoner status; for Austria-Hungary 32%; for Italy 26%; for France 12%; for Germany 9%; for Britain 7%. Prisoners from the Allied armies totalled about 1.4 million (not including Russia, which lost 2.5–3.5 million soldiers as prisoners). From the Central Powers, about 3.3 million soldiers became prisoners; most of them surrendered to Russians.[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerguson1999368–369-278)

## Soldiers' experiences

Allied personnel was around 42,928,000, while Central personnel was near 25,248,000.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts2005[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid2YqjfHLyyj8CpgPA273_273]-220) British soldiers of the war were initially volunteers but were increasingly [conscripted](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription"). Surviving veterans returning home often found they could discuss their experiences only among themselves, so formed "veterans' associations" or "Legions".

### Conscription

Further information: [Conscription Crisis of 1917](/wiki/Conscription_Crisis_of_1917 "Conscription Crisis of 1917"), [Conscription Crisis of 1918](/wiki/Conscription_Crisis_of_1918 "Conscription Crisis of 1918"), [World War I conscription in Australia](/wiki/World_War_I_conscription_in_Australia "World War I conscription in Australia"), [Recruitment to the British Army during World War I](/wiki/Recruitment_to_the_British_Army_during_World_War_I "Recruitment to the British Army during World War I"), and [Conscription in the United States § World War I](/wiki/Conscription_in_the_United_States#World_War_I "Conscription in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/I_want_you_for_U.S._Army_3b48465u_edit.jpg/250px-I_want_you_for_U.S._Army_3b48465u_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:I_want_you_for_U.S._Army_3b48465u_edit.jpg)

U.S. Army recruiting poster with [Uncle Sam](/wiki/Uncle_Sam "Uncle Sam"), 1917

Conscription was common in most European countries, but was controversial in English-speaking countries.[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHavighurst1985131-279) It was especially unpopular among minority ethnicities, especially the Irish Catholics in Ireland,[[273]](#cite_note-280) Australia,[[274]](#cite_note-281)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[275]](#cite_note-parl-282) and the French Catholics in Canada.[[276]](#cite_note-283)[[277]](#cite_note-284)

In the US, conscription began in 1917; while the country avoided the outright draft riots that occurred during the Civil War, and was able to splinter opposition through heavy pressure and sometimes outright repression, resistance was nonetheless considerable, particularly in rural areas and in the South.[[278]](#cite_note-285) The administration decided to rely primarily on conscription, rather than voluntary enlistment, to raise military manpower after only 73,000 volunteers enlisted out of the initial 1 million target in the first six weeks of war.[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZinn2003134-286)

### Military attachés and war correspondents

Main articles: [List of military attachés in World War I](/wiki/List_of_military_attach%C3%A9s_in_World_War_I "List of military attachés in World War I") and [List of military attachés and war correspondents in World War I](/wiki/List_of_military_attach%C3%A9s_and_war_correspondents_in_World_War_I "List of military attachés and war correspondents in World War I")

Military and civilian observers from every major power closely followed the course of the war.[[280]](#cite_note-287) Many were able to report on events from a perspective somewhat akin to modern "[embedded](/wiki/Embedded_journalism "Embedded journalism")" positions within the opposing land and naval forces.[[281]](#cite_note-288)[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECraig1949-289)

## Economic effects

Main articles: [Economic history of World War I](/wiki/Economic_history_of_World_War_I "Economic history of World War I") and [Post–World War I recession](/wiki/Post%E2%80%93World_War_I_recession "Post–World War I recession")

Further information: [Home front during World War I](/wiki/Home_front_during_World_War_I "Home front during World War I") and [Financial crisis of 1914](/wiki/Financial_crisis_of_1914 "Financial crisis of 1914")

Macro- and micro-economic consequences devolved from the war. Families were altered by the departure of many men. With the death or absence of the primary wage earner, women were forced into the workforce in unprecedented numbers. At the same time, the industry needed to replace the lost labourers sent to war. The movement of women into industry and traditionally male labor ultimately aided the struggle for [voting rights for women](/wiki/Suffragette "Suffragette").[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENoakes200648-290)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/The_Girl_Behind_the_Gun_1915.jpg/250px-The_Girl_Behind_the_Gun_1915.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Girl_Behind_the_Gun_1915.jpg)

Poster showing women workers, 1915

In all nations, the government's share of GDP increased, surpassing 50% in both Germany and France and nearly reaching that level in Britain. To pay for purchases in the US, Britain cashed in its extensive investments in American railroads and then began borrowing heavily from [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street"). President Wilson was on the verge of cutting off the loans in late 1916 but allowed a great increase in [US government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") lending to the Allies. After 1919, the US demanded repayment of these loans. The repayments were, in part, funded by German reparations that, in turn, were supported by American loans to Germany. This circular system collapsed in 1931 and some loans were never repaid. Britain still owed the United States $4.4 [billion](/wiki/1,000,000,000 "1,000,000,000")[[h]](#cite_note-291) of World War I debt in 1934; the last installment was finally paid in 2015.[[284]](#cite_note-292)

Britain turned to her colonies for help in obtaining essential war materials whose supply from traditional sources had become difficult. Geologists such as [Albert Kitson](/wiki/Albert_Kitson "Albert Kitson") were called on to find new resources of precious minerals in the African colonies. Kitson discovered important new deposits of [manganese](/wiki/Manganese "Manganese"), used in munitions production, in the [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast_(region) "Gold Coast (region)").[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreen1938cxxvi-293)

Article 231 of the Treaty of Versailles (the so-called "war guilt" clause) stated Germany accepted responsibility for "all the loss and damage to which the Allied and Associated Governments and their nationals have been subjected as a consequence of the war imposed upon them by the aggression of Germany and her allies."[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaesJay19948-294) It was worded as such to lay a legal basis for reparations, and a similar clause was inserted in the treaties with Austria and Hungary. However, neither of them interpreted it as an admission of war guilt.[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarks1978231–232-295) In 1921, the total reparation sum was placed at 132 billion gold marks. However, "Allied experts knew that Germany could not pay" this sum. The total sum was divided into three categories, with the third being "deliberately designed to be chimerical" and its "primary function was to mislead public opinion ... into believing the 'total sum was being maintained.'"[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarks1978237-296) Thus, 50 billion gold marks (12.5 billion dollars) "represented the actual Allied assessment of German capacity to pay" and "therefore ... represented the total German reparations" figure that had to be paid.[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarks1978237-296)

This figure could be paid in cash or in-kind (coal, timber, chemical dyes, etc.). Some of the territory lost—via the Treaty of Versailles—was credited towards the reparation figure as were other acts such as helping to restore the Library of Louvain.[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarks1978223–234-297) By 1929, the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") caused political chaos throughout the world.[[290]](#cite_note-298) In 1932 the payment of reparations was suspended by the international community, by which point Germany had paid only the equivalent of 20.598 billion gold marks.[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarks1978233-299) With the rise of [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler"), all bonds and loans that had been issued and taken out during the 1920s and early 1930s were cancelled. [David Andelman](/wiki/David_A._Andelman "David A. Andelman") notes "Refusing to pay doesn't make an agreement null and void. The bonds, the agreement, still exist." Thus, following the Second World War, at the [London Conference](/wiki/London_Agreement_on_German_External_Debts "London Agreement on German External Debts") in 1953, Germany agreed to resume payment on the money borrowed. On 3 October 2010, Germany made the final payment on these bonds.[[i]](#cite_note-304)

The Australian prime minister, [Billy Hughes](/wiki/Billy_Hughes "Billy Hughes"), wrote to the British prime minister, [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George"), "You have assured us that you cannot get better terms. I much regret it, and hope even now that some way may be found of securing agreement for demanding reparation commensurate with the tremendous sacrifices made by the British Empire and her Allies." Australia received £5,571,720 in war reparations, but the direct cost of the war to Australia had been £376,993,052, and, by the mid-1930s, repatriation pensions, war gratuities, interest and sinking fund charges were £831,280,947.[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouter2000354-305)

## Support and opposition for the war

### Support

Further information: [Propaganda in World War I](/wiki/Propaganda_in_World_War_I "Propaganda in World War I"), [British propaganda during World War I](/wiki/British_propaganda_during_World_War_I "British propaganda during World War I"), and [Propaganda and censorship in Italy during the First World War](/wiki/Propaganda_and_censorship_in_Italy_during_the_First_World_War "Propaganda and censorship in Italy during the First World War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Affiche-guerre_Femmes-au-travail.jpg/250px-Affiche-guerre_Femmes-au-travail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Affiche-guerre_Femmes-au-travail.jpg)

Poster urging women to join the British war effort, published by the [Young Women's Christian Association](/wiki/YWCA "YWCA"), 1915

In the Balkans, [Yugoslav nationalists](/wiki/Yugoslavism "Yugoslavism") such as the leader, [Ante Trumbić](/wiki/Ante_Trumbi%C4%87 "Ante Trumbić"), strongly supported the war, desiring the freedom of [Yugoslavs](/wiki/Yugoslavs "Yugoslavs") from Austria-Hungary and other foreign powers and the creation of an independent Yugoslavia. The [Yugoslav Committee](/wiki/Yugoslav_Committee "Yugoslav Committee"), led by Trumbić, was formed in Paris on 30 April 1915, but shortly moved its office to London.[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts20051189-306) In April 1918, the Rome Congress of Oppressed Nationalities met, including [Czechoslovak](/wiki/Czechoslovakism "Czechoslovakism"), Italian, [Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people"), [Transylvanian](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"), and Yugoslav representatives who urged the Allies to support national [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination") for the peoples residing within Austria-Hungary.[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts20051001-307)

In the Middle East, [Arab nationalism](/wiki/Arab_nationalism "Arab nationalism") soared in Ottoman territories in response to the rise of Turkish nationalism during the war. Arab nationalist leaders advocated the creation of a [pan-Arab](/wiki/Pan-Arabism "Pan-Arabism") state. In 1916, the Arab Revolt began in Ottoman-controlled territories of the Middle East to achieve independence.[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts2005117-308)

In East Africa, [Iyasu V](/wiki/Lij_Iyasu_of_Ethiopia "Lij Iyasu of Ethiopia") of [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopian_Empire "Ethiopian Empire") was supporting the [Dervish state](/wiki/Dervish_movement_(Somali) "Dervish movement (Somali)") who were at war with the British in the [Somaliland campaign](/wiki/Somaliland_campaign "Somaliland campaign").[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMukhtar2003126-309) Von Syburg, the German envoy in [Addis Ababa](/wiki/Addis_Ababa "Addis Ababa"), said, "now the time has come for Ethiopia to regain the coast of the Red Sea driving the Italians home, to restore the Empire to its ancient size." The Ethiopian Empire was on the verge of entering World War I on the side of the Central Powers before Iyasu's overthrow at the [Battle of Segale](/wiki/Battle_of_Segale "Battle of Segale") due to Allied pressure on the Ethiopian aristocracy.[[301]](#cite_note-310)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/BVRC-Great-War-Contingent_1914.jpg/250px-BVRC-Great-War-Contingent_1914.jpg)](/wiki/File:BVRC-Great-War-Contingent_1914.jpg)

[Bermuda Volunteer Rifle Corps](/wiki/Bermuda_Volunteer_Rifle_Corps "Bermuda Volunteer Rifle Corps") First Contingent in Bermuda, winter 1914–1915, before joining [1 Lincolnshire Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Lincolnshire_Regiment "Royal Lincolnshire Regiment") in France in June 1915. The dozen remaining after [Guedecourt](/wiki/Capture_of_Gueudecourt "Capture of Gueudecourt") on 25 September 1916, merged with a Second Contingent. The two contingents suffered 75% casualties.

Several socialist parties initially supported the war when it began in August 1914.[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts20051001-307) But European socialists split on national lines, with the concept of [class conflict](/wiki/Class_conflict "Class conflict") held by radical socialists such as Marxists and [syndicalists](/wiki/Syndicalism "Syndicalism") being overborne by their patriotic support for the war.[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts20051069-311) Once the war began, Austrian, British, French, German, and Russian socialists followed the rising nationalist current by supporting their countries' intervention in the war.[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts2005884-312)

[Italian nationalism](/wiki/Italian_nationalism "Italian nationalism") was stirred by the outbreak of the war and was initially strongly supported by a variety of political factions. One of the most prominent and popular Italian nationalist supporters of the war was [Gabriele D'Annunzio](/wiki/Gabriele_D%27Annunzio "Gabriele D'Annunzio"), who promoted [Italian irredentism](/wiki/Italian_irredentism "Italian irredentism") and helped sway the Italian public to support intervention in the war.[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts2005335-313) The [Italian Liberal Party](/wiki/Italian_Liberal_Party "Italian Liberal Party"), under the leadership of [Paolo Boselli](/wiki/Paolo_Boselli "Paolo Boselli"), promoted intervention in the war on the side of the Allies and used the Dante Alighieri Society to promote Italian nationalism.[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts2005219-314) Italian socialists were divided on whether to support the war or oppose it; some were militant supporters of the war, including [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") and [Leonida Bissolati](/wiki/Leonida_Bissolati "Leonida Bissolati").[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts2005209-315) However, the [Italian Socialist Party](/wiki/Italian_Socialist_Party "Italian Socialist Party") decided to oppose the war after anti-militarist protestors were killed, resulting in a [general strike](/wiki/General_strike "General strike") called [Red Week](/wiki/Red_Week_(Italy) "Red Week (Italy)").[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts2005596-316) The Italian Socialist Party purged itself of pro-war nationalist members, including Mussolini.[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts2005596-316) Mussolini formed the pro-interventionist *[Il Popolo d'Italia](/wiki/Il_Popolo_d%27Italia "Il Popolo d'Italia")* and the *Fasci Rivoluzionario d'Azione Internazionalista* ("Revolutionary [Fasci](/wiki/Fascio "Fascio") for International Action") in October 1914 that later developed into the *[Fasci Italiani di Combattimento](/wiki/Fasci_Italiani_di_Combattimento "Fasci Italiani di Combattimento")* in 1919, the origin of fascism.[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts2005826-317) Mussolini's nationalism enabled him to raise funds from [Ansaldo](/wiki/Gio._Ansaldo_%26_C. "Gio. Ansaldo & C.") (an armaments firm) and other companies to create *Il Popolo d'Italia* to convince socialists and revolutionaries to support the war.[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMack_Smith1997284-318)

#### Patriotic funds

On both sides, there was large-scale fundraising for soldiers' welfare, their dependents and those injured. The [Nail Men](/wiki/Nail_Men "Nail Men") were a German example. Around the British Empire, there were many patriotic funds, including the [Royal Patriotic Fund Corporation](/wiki/Royal_Patriotic_Fund_Corporation "Royal Patriotic Fund Corporation"), [Canadian Patriotic Fund](/wiki/Canadian_Patriotic_Fund "Canadian Patriotic Fund"), [Queensland Patriotic Fund](/wiki/Queensland_Patriotic_Fund "Queensland Patriotic Fund") and, by 1919, there were 983 funds in New Zealand.[[310]](#cite_note-319) At the start of the next world war the New Zealand funds were reformed, having been criticised as overlapping, wasteful and abused,[[311]](#cite_note-320) but 11 were still functioning in 2002.[[312]](#cite_note-321)

### Opposition

Main articles: [Opposition to World War I](/wiki/Opposition_to_World_War_I "Opposition to World War I") and [1917 French Army mutinies](/wiki/1917_French_Army_mutinies "1917 French Army mutinies")

Many countries jailed those who spoke out against the conflict. These included [Eugene Debs](/wiki/Eugene_V._Debs "Eugene V. Debs") in the US and [Bertrand Russell](/wiki/Bertrand_Russell "Bertrand Russell") in Britain. In the US, the [Espionage Act of 1917](/wiki/Espionage_Act_of_1917 "Espionage Act of 1917") and [Sedition Act of 1918](/wiki/Sedition_Act_of_1918 "Sedition Act of 1918") made it a federal crime to oppose military recruitment or make any statements deemed "disloyal". Publications at all critical of the government were removed from circulation by postal censors,[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKarp1979-322) [*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] and many served long prison sentences for statements of fact deemed unpatriotic.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Sackville_Street_%28Dublin%29_after_the_1916_Easter_Rising.JPG/250px-Sackville_Street_%28Dublin%29_after_the_1916_Easter_Rising.JPG)](/wiki/File:Sackville_Street_(Dublin)_after_the_1916_Easter_Rising.JPG)

Sackville Street (now [O'Connell Street](/wiki/O%27Connell_Street "O'Connell Street")) after the 1916 [Easter Rising](/wiki/Easter_Rising "Easter Rising") in Dublin

Several nationalists opposed intervention, particularly within states that the nationalists were hostile to. Although the vast majority of Irish people consented to participate in the war in 1914 and 1915, a minority of advanced [Irish nationalists](/wiki/Irish_nationalism "Irish nationalism") had staunchly opposed taking part.[[314]](#cite_note-323)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] The war began amid the Home Rule crisis in Ireland that had resurfaced in 1912, and by July 1914 there was a serious possibility of an outbreak of civil war in Ireland. Irish nationalists and Marxists attempted to pursue Irish independence, culminating in the [Easter Rising](/wiki/Easter_Rising "Easter Rising") of 1916, with Germany sending 20,000 rifles to Ireland to stir unrest in Britain.[[315]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckerRoberts2005584-324) The British government placed Ireland under [martial law](/wiki/Martial_law "Martial law") in response to the Easter Rising, though once the immediate threat of revolution had dissipated, the authorities did try to make concessions to nationalist feeling.[[316]](#cite_note-325)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] However, opposition to involvement in the war increased in Ireland, resulting in the [Conscription Crisis of 1918](/wiki/Conscription_Crisis_of_1918 "Conscription Crisis of 1918").

Other opposition came from [conscientious objectors](/wiki/Conscientious_objector "Conscientious objector")—some socialist, some religious—who had refused to fight. In Britain, 16,000 people asked for conscientious objector status.[[317]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELehmannvan_der_Veer199962-326) Some of them, most notably prominent peace activist [Stephen Hobhouse](/wiki/Stephen_Hobhouse "Stephen Hobhouse"), refused both military and [alternative service](/wiki/Alternative_civilian_service "Alternative civilian service").[[318]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrock200414-327) Many suffered years of prison, including [solitary confinement](/wiki/Solitary_confinement "Solitary confinement"). Even after the war, in Britain, many job advertisements were marked "No conscientious objectors need to apply".[[319]](#cite_note-328)

On 1–4 May 1917, about 100,000 workers and soldiers of [Petrograd](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg"), and after them, the workers and soldiers of other Russian cities, led by the Bolsheviks, demonstrated under banners reading "Down with the war!" and "all power to the Soviets!". The mass demonstrations resulted in a crisis for the [Russian Provisional Government](/wiki/Russian_Provisional_Government "Russian Provisional Government").[[320]](#cite_note-329) In [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), in May 1917, Bolshevik revolutionaries organised and engaged in rioting calling for an end to the war, and managed to close down factories and stop public transportation.[[321]](#cite_note-Seton_6-330) The Italian army was forced to enter Milan with tanks and machine guns to face Bolsheviks and [anarchists](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism"), who fought violently until 23 May when the army gained control of the city. Almost 50 people (including three Italian soldiers) were killed and over 800 people were arrested.[[321]](#cite_note-Seton_6-330)

## Technology

See also: [Technology during World War I](/wiki/Technology_during_World_War_I "Technology during World War I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/British_Aircraft_of_the_First_World_War_1914-1918_%28IWM_Q67556%29.jpg/250px-British_Aircraft_of_the_First_World_War_1914-1918_%28IWM_Q67556%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_Aircraft_of_the_First_World_War_1914-1918_(IWM_Q67556).jpg)

[Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") [Sopwith Camel](/wiki/Sopwith_Camel "Sopwith Camel"). In April 1917, the average life expectancy of a British pilot on the Western Front was 93 flying hours.[[322]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELawsonLawson2002123-331)

World War I began as a clash of 20th-century technology and 19th-century [tactics](/wiki/Military_tactics "Military tactics"), with the inevitably large ensuing casualties. By the end of 1917, however, the major armies had modernised and were making use of telephone, [wireless communication](/wiki/Wireless "Wireless"),[[323]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHartcup1988154-332) [armoured cars](/wiki/Armored_car_(military) "Armored car (military)"), [tanks](/wiki/Tank "Tank") (especially with the advent of the prototype tank, [Little Willie](/wiki/Little_Willie "Little Willie")), and aircraft.[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHartcup198882–86-333)
