Several types of poison gas were used by both sides. Although it never proved a decisive weapon, it became one of the most feared of the war.[[87]](#cite_note-:0-90)[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELove1996-91)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Marcel_Louis_Courmes_en_fourrure_1915.jpg/250px-Marcel_Louis_Courmes_en_fourrure_1915.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marcel_Louis_Courmes_en_fourrure_1915.jpg)

[Captain Marcel Courmes](/wiki/Marcel_Courmes "Marcel Courmes"), pilot of the French 2nd Bombardment, Group GB 2, in August 1915

Artillery also underwent a revolution. In 1914, cannons were positioned in the front line and fired directly at their targets. By 1917, [indirect fire](/wiki/Indirect_fire "Indirect fire") with guns (as well as mortars and even machine guns) was commonplace, using new techniques for spotting and ranging, notably, aircraft and the [field telephone](/wiki/Field_telephone "Field telephone").[[325]](#cite_note-334)

[Fixed-wing aircraft](/wiki/Fixed-wing_aircraft "Fixed-wing aircraft") were initially used for [reconnaissance](/wiki/Reconnaissance "Reconnaissance") and [ground attack](/wiki/Close_air_support "Close air support"). To shoot down enemy planes, [anti-aircraft guns](/wiki/Anti-aircraft_warfare "Anti-aircraft warfare") and [fighter aircraft](/wiki/Fighter_aircraft "Fighter aircraft") were developed. [Strategic bombers](/wiki/Strategic_bomber "Strategic bomber") were created, principally by the Germans and British, though the former used [Zeppelins](/wiki/Zeppelin "Zeppelin") as well.[[326]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECross1991-335) Towards the end of the conflict, [aircraft carriers](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier") were used for the first time, with HMS *Furious* launching [Sopwith Camels](/wiki/Sopwith_Camel "Sopwith Camel") in a raid to destroy the Zeppelin hangars at [Tønder](/wiki/T%C3%B8nder "Tønder") in 1918.[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECross199156–57-336)

## Diplomacy

Main article: [Diplomatic history of World War I](/wiki/Diplomatic_history_of_World_War_I "Diplomatic history of World War I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Cartoon_for_a_Telegram.jpg/250px-Cartoon_for_a_Telegram.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cartoon_for_a_Telegram.jpg)

1917 political cartoon about the [Zimmermann Telegram](/wiki/Zimmermann_Telegram "Zimmermann Telegram")

The non-military diplomatic and propaganda interactions among the nations were designed to build support for the cause or to undermine support for the enemy. For the most part, wartime diplomacy focused on five issues: [propaganda campaigns](/wiki/Propaganda_in_World_War_I "Propaganda in World War I"); defining and redefining the war goals, which became harsher as the war went on; luring neutral nations (Italy, Ottoman Empire, Bulgaria, Romania) into the coalition by offering slices of enemy territory; and encouragement by the Allies of nationalistic minority movements inside the Central Powers, especially among Czechs, Poles, and Arabs. In addition, multiple peace proposals were coming from neutrals, or one side or the other; none of them progressed very far.[[328]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStevenson1988[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_July_2020]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (July_2020)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-337)[[329]](#cite_note-338)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[330]](#cite_note-339)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

## Legacy and memory

Main articles: [List of last surviving World War I veterans](/wiki/List_of_last_surviving_World_War_I_veterans "List of last surviving World War I veterans"), [Commonwealth War Graves Commission](/wiki/Commonwealth_War_Graves_Commission "Commonwealth War Graves Commission"), and [American Battle Monuments Commission](/wiki/American_Battle_Monuments_Commission "American Battle Monuments Commission")

Further information: [World War I in popular culture](/wiki/World_War_I_in_popular_culture "World War I in popular culture")

### Memorials

Main article: [World War I memorials](/wiki/World_War_I_memorials "World War I memorials")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Sacrario_militare_di_Redipuglia_agosto_2014.JPG/250px-Sacrario_militare_di_Redipuglia_agosto_2014.JPG)](/wiki/File:Sacrario_militare_di_Redipuglia_agosto_2014.JPG)

The Italian [Redipuglia War Memorial](/wiki/Redipuglia_War_Memorial "Redipuglia War Memorial"), which contains the remains of 100,187 soldiers

Memorials were built in thousands of villages and towns. Close to battlefields, those buried in improvised burial grounds were gradually moved to formal graveyards under the care of organisations such as the [Commonwealth War Graves Commission](/wiki/Commonwealth_War_Graves_Commission "Commonwealth War Graves Commission"), the [American Battle Monuments Commission](/wiki/American_Battle_Monuments_Commission "American Battle Monuments Commission"), the [German War Graves Commission](/wiki/German_War_Graves_Commission "German War Graves Commission"), and [Le Souvenir français](/wiki/Le_Souvenir_fran%C3%A7ais "Le Souvenir français"). Many of these graveyards also have monuments to the missing or [unidentified](/wiki/Tomb_of_the_Unknown_Soldier "Tomb of the Unknown Soldier") dead, such as the [Menin Gate Memorial to the Missing](/wiki/Menin_Gate "Menin Gate") and the [Thiepval Memorial to the Missing of the Somme](/wiki/Thiepval_Memorial "Thiepval Memorial").[[331]](#cite_note-340)[[332]](#cite_note-341)

In 1915, [John McCrae](/wiki/John_McCrae "John McCrae"), a Canadian army doctor, wrote the poem *[In Flanders Fields](/wiki/In_Flanders_Fields "In Flanders Fields")* as a salute to those who perished in the war. It is still recited today, especially on [Remembrance Day](/wiki/Armistice_Day "Armistice Day") and [Memorial Day](/wiki/Memorial_Day "Memorial Day").[[333]](#cite_note-342)[[334]](#cite_note-343)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Pagny_le_Chateau_monument_morts_002b.jpg/250px-Pagny_le_Chateau_monument_morts_002b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pagny_le_Chateau_monument_morts_002b.jpg)

A typical village [war memorial](/wiki/War_memorial "War memorial") to soldiers killed in World War I

[National World War I Museum and Memorial](/wiki/National_World_War_I_Museum_and_Memorial "National World War I Museum and Memorial") in [Kansas City, Missouri](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri"), is a memorial dedicated to all Americans who served in World War I. The [Liberty Memorial](/wiki/National_World_War_I_Museum_and_Memorial "National World War I Museum and Memorial") was dedicated on 1 November 1921.[[335]](#cite_note-kclibrary.org-344)

The British government budgeted substantial resources to [the commemoration of the war during the period 2014 to 2018](/wiki/First_World_War_centenary "First World War centenary"). The lead body is the [Imperial War Museum](/wiki/Imperial_War_Museum "Imperial War Museum").[[336]](#cite_note-345) On 3 August 2014, French President [François Hollande](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Hollande "François Hollande") and German President [Joachim Gauck](/wiki/Joachim_Gauck "Joachim Gauck") together marked [the centenary of Germany's declaration of war on France](/wiki/Centenary_of_the_outbreak_of_World_War_I "Centenary of the outbreak of World War I") by laying the first stone of a memorial in Vieil Armand, known in German as [Hartmannswillerkopf](/wiki/Hartmannswillerkopf "Hartmannswillerkopf"), for French and German soldiers killed in the war.[[337]](#cite_note-HartmannswillerkopfMemorial-346) As part of commemorations for the [centenary of the 1918 Armistice](/wiki/Centenary_of_the_Armistice_of_11_November_1918 "Centenary of the Armistice of 11 November 1918"), French President [Emmanuel Macron](/wiki/Emmanuel_Macron "Emmanuel Macron") and German Chancellor [Angela Merkel](/wiki/Angela_Merkel "Angela Merkel") visited the site of the signing of the Armistice of Compiègne and unveiled a plaque to reconciliation.[[338]](#cite_note-347)

### Historiography

Main article: [Historiography of World War I](/wiki/Historiography_of_World_War_I "Historiography of World War I")

Further information: [Historiography of the causes of World War I](/wiki/Historiography_of_the_causes_of_World_War_I "Historiography of the causes of World War I")

> ... "Strange, friend," I said, "Here is no cause to mourn."  
> "None," said the other, "Save the undone years" ...

— [Wilfred Owen](/wiki/Wilfred_Owen "Wilfred Owen"), *Strange Meeting*, 1918[[339]](#cite_note-Wilfred_Owen_2004-348)

The first efforts to comprehend the meaning and consequences of [modern warfare](/wiki/Modern_warfare "Modern warfare") began during the initial phases of the war and are still underway more than a century later. Teaching World War I has presented special challenges. When compared with [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the First World War is often thought to be "a wrong war fought for the wrong reasons"; it lacks the [metanarrative](/wiki/Metanarrative "Metanarrative") of [good versus evil](/wiki/Good_and_evil "Good and evil") that characterises retellings of the Second World War. Lacking recognisable heroes and villains, it is often taught thematically, invoking simplified [tropes](/wiki/Trope_(literature) "Trope (literature)") that obscure the complexity of the conflict.[[340]](#cite_note-Neiberg2-349)

Historian Heather Jones argues that the [historiography](/wiki/Historiography "Historiography") has been reinvigorated by a cultural turn in the 21st century. Scholars have raised entirely new questions regarding [military occupation](/wiki/Military_occupation "Military occupation"), [radicalisation](/wiki/Radicalization "Radicalization") of politics, [race](/wiki/Race_(human_categorization) "Race (human categorization)"), [medical science](/wiki/Medical_science "Medical science"), [gender](/wiki/Gender_history "Gender history") and [mental health](/wiki/Mental_health "Mental health"). Among the major subjects that historians have long debated regarding the war include [why the war began](/wiki/Historiography_of_the_causes_of_World_War_I "Historiography of the causes of World War I"), why the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") won, whether generals were responsible for [high casualty rates](/wiki/World_War_I_casualties "World War I casualties"), how soldiers endured the poor conditions of [trench warfare](/wiki/Trench_warfare "Trench warfare"), and to what extent the civilian [home front](/wiki/Home_front_during_World_War_I "Home front during World War I") accepted and endorsed the war effort.[[341]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones2013858-350)[[342]](#cite_note-351)

### Unexploded ordnance

Further information: [Zone rouge](/wiki/Zone_rouge "Zone rouge") and [Iron harvest](/wiki/Iron_harvest "Iron harvest")

As late as 2007, [unexploded ordnance](/wiki/Unexploded_ordnance "Unexploded ordnance") at battlefield sites like [Verdun](/wiki/Verdun "Verdun") and [Somme](/wiki/Somme_(department) "Somme (department)") continued to pose a danger. In France and Belgium, locals who discover caches of unexploded munitions are assisted by weapons disposal units. In some places, plant life has still not recovered from the effects of the war.[[340]](#cite_note-Neiberg2-349)

* [International Encyclopedia of the First World War](http://www.1914-1918-online.net/#:~:text=International%20Encyclopedia%20of%20the%20First%20World%20War%20%E2%80%9D,authors%2C%20editors%2C%20and%20partners%20from%20over%20fifty%20countries.)

* European Newspapers from the [start of the First World War](https://www.theeuropeanlibrary.org/tel4/newspapers/search?query=&decade=1910-1919&month=7&year=1914&&count=50) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20160115140047/http://www.theeuropeanlibrary.org/tel4/newspapers/search?query=&decade=1910-1919&month=7&year=1914&&count=50) 15 January 2016 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine") and the [end of the war](https://www.theeuropeanlibrary.org/tel4/newspapers/search?query=&decade=1910-1919&month=11&year=1918&count=50) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20150405154114/http://www.theeuropeanlibrary.org/tel4/newspapers/search?query=&decade=1910-1919&month=11&year=1918&count=50) 5 April 2015 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine") from [The European Library](/wiki/The_European_Library "The European Library")
* [WWI Films](http://www.europeanfilmgateway.eu/node/33/efg1914/multilingual%3A1) on the European Film Gateway
* [The British Pathé WW1 Film Archive](http://www.britishpathe.com/workspaces/page/ww1-the-definitive-collection) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20190324234810/http://www.britishpathe.com/workspaces/page/ww1-the-definitive-collection) 24 March 2019 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")
* [World War I British press photograph collection](https://open.library.ubc.ca/collections/wwiphoto) – A sampling of images distributed by the British government during the war to diplomats overseas, from the [University of British Columbia](/wiki/University_of_British_Columbia "University of British Columbia") Library Digital Collections
* [Personal accounts of American World War I veterans](http://memory.loc.gov/diglib/vhp/search?query=&field=all&war=worldwari), Veterans History Project, [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress")
* [WWI Pamphlets 1913-1920](https://archive.org/details/butlerlibrarywwipamphlets) — A collection of WWI Pamphlets 1913-1920 contributed by [Columbia University Libraries](/wiki/Columbia_University_Libraries "Columbia University Libraries"), available online on [Internet Archive](/wiki/Internet_Archive "Internet Archive")
* [World War I manuscript collection](https://digital.shsmo.org/digital/collection/wwi) from [The State Historical Society of Missouri](/wiki/The_State_Historical_Society_of_Missouri "The State Historical Society of Missouri") Digitised Collections
